  single project: any predictive subset blocks adaptation of the
  whole.
- The only valid mixing is **product decomposition into independent
  parts** delivered as separate projects, each pure-approach. That
  is a *program*, not a hybrid project.
- "Hybrid" labels often arise from cargo-cult mixing of
  surface-level practices (PMBOK 7 still includes the term despite
  the author's objection).

## Project life cycle

- The PM **life cycle** must match the development approach.
    - **Cyclic** PM life cycle works for both adaptive and (more
      or less) linear predictive development.
    - **Linear** PM life cycle works only for predictive.
- Some methods support both approaches (P3.express); some support
  only adaptive (DSDM). Pick a methodology that matches the
  development approach.

## Product life cycle

- The project life cycle is a *subset* of the product life cycle.
- Project decisions must consider the product's wider context
  (e.g. spend €50k more for €2k/month operating-cost savings →
  decision belongs at customer-org level, but project should
  surface the option).

## When this domain matters more

- Choosing a methodology for a new project
- Resisting fashion-driven Agile rollouts on inappropriate projects
- Composing programs that span both predictive and adaptive parts

## Anti-patterns

- "Agile by default" / "Predictive by default" without product analysis
- Team / org preference as decision driver
- Calling a mix of practices "hybrid" without examining dynamics
- Using adaptive without true incremental delivery
- Mismatched PM life cycle vs development approach

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §11 [Adaptability](../principles/pmbok-7/11-adaptability.md)
- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../principles/pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)
- Methodology selection: [Methodologies](../methodologies/index.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/03-development-approach-and-life-cycle.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/03-development-approach-and-life-cycle.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/performance-domains/04-planning.md -->
<!-- title: Planning (performance domain) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Planning — performance domain

**TL;DR:** All projects plan — adaptive ones plan *differently*, not
*less*. Plan continually, cover all 10 knowledge areas, separate
**plan** (artifacts) from **meta-plan** (how-to-plan).

## Planning per development approach

| Approach | Up-front plan | Detail timing |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Predictive | detailed *or* high-level | full upfront, or per stage |
| Adaptive | very high-level only | full only just before execution |

**Why we plan:** to direct work, and to enable monitor-and-control.
A plan is judged in the context of its development approach and
methodology — same plan can be good in one setup, bad in another.

## Continual planning

- Plan once, never revise → useless plan.
- All approaches require continuous re-planning.

## What gets planned (10 knowledge areas, from PMBOK 6 era)

A reusable checklist:
1. Integration
2. Scope
3. Schedule
4. Cost
5. Quality
6. Resources
7. Communications
8. Risk
9. Procurement
10. Stakeholders

Diagnostic: when someone shows you "the plan" and it is just a
schedule, ask where the other 9 areas are planned.

## Schedule types

- **Dependency-based** — Critical Path Method (CPM) or a similar
  method. For projects with hard precedence (build floor → wall →
  paint). Tools: Primavera P6, MS Project.
- **Priority-based.** For projects whose elements aren't
  strongly dependent — pick by priority and work through.
- **Adaptive projects require priority-based.** If you can't
  produce a true priority-based schedule, the product probably
  shouldn't be developed adaptively.
- Predictive can use either type.

## Levels: plans and meta-plans

- **Plan** = the artifact (e.g. a *risk register* listing risks and
  responses).
- **Meta-plan** = how you'll plan/monitor/control (e.g. a *risk
  management plan* describing techniques, workflows, artifacts).
- Methodologies embed some meta-planning. PRINCE2 expects explicit
  meta-plans; P3.express does not but you can add them where
  attention is needed (e.g. a *procurement strategy* if procurement
  is sensitive).
- Centralize meta-plans for reuse across projects (a *Center of
  Excellence* or *PMO*). Projects adjust them; useful general
  adjustments feed back to the center. This is the
  organization-level step of PMBOK 7's two-step tailoring.
- Caveat: "PMO" is a vague term. Don't let a PMO duplicate or
  collide with project-level PM.

## When this domain matters more

- Long-running, regulated, multi-team projects
- Initial methodology rollout (build the meta-plan library)
- Drafting an enterprise PMO scope and remit

## Anti-patterns

- Calling a Gantt "the plan"
- Big upfront plan, never updated
- "Agile = no planning"
- Single PMO doing project-level work
- Identical detail across all knowledge areas regardless of
  project sensitivity

## Cross-refs

- PRINCE2 §4 [Manage by stages](../principles/prince2/04-manage-by-stages.md)
- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../principles/pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](05-project-work.md), [Measurement](06-measurement.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/04-planning.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/04-planning.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/performance-domains/05-project-work.md -->
<!-- title: Project Work (performance domain) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Project Work — performance domain

**TL;DR:** Plan first, then work — but with a constant *two-way
dance* between plans and reality. Capture **follow-up items** as
they emerge; never let them slip.

## Plan-work balance

Three modes:
- **Working without a plan.** Team improvises against a vague
  mental image. Plan (if any) is updated *after* the fact, only
  one direction.
- **Working without flexibility.** Fixed detailed plan rigidly
  enforced. Unrealistic in any complex/complicated project.
- **Balanced (right).** Plans direct work; observed reality feeds
  back into plans. Continuous adjustment in both directions.

Concrete example: P3.express weekly kick-off — team reviews the
upcoming week to confirm the plan is still the best path and there
are no conflicts.

When plans change → measurements re-run → corrective or preventive
actions decided.

Strengthen the methodology in this area when projects are
high-uncertainty.

## Level of detail

- Management-layer plans should stop at *appropriate* detail; leave
  the rest to teams. Too much detail in management plans →
  inflexibility.
- Example layering (PRINCE2):
    - high-level plan upfront
    - stage plan immediately before each stage
    - **team plan** owned by the team, not management
- Trivial details belong in ad-hoc team decisions. Don't plan them.

## Follow-up items (P3.express term, broadly applicable)

Five things worth tracking in a single artifact (P3.express
*follow-up register*) — or in multiple artifacts in heavier methods:

1. **Risks** — uncertainties for the future.
   - Daily stand-up Q3 ("What problems *might* I have?") in Agile
     surfaces them. Adapt the cadence to project size.
2. **Issues** — unplanned things that already happened.
3. **Improvement plans** — outputs of satisfaction evaluations and
   retrospectives.
4. **Change requests** — assess impact (time/cost/quality/...),
   prepare proposals, requester picks. Change is fine *when
   managed*; the bug is unmanaged change.
5. **Lessons learned** — captured during the project, not just at
   close.

**Custodian rule (P3.express).** Every follow-up item *and* every
deliverable should have a named custodian who tracks it through to
closure.

Document items as soon as discovered — undocumented items consume
mental energy and risk being forgotten.

## When this domain matters more

- High-uncertainty projects
- Projects with frequent change requests or evolving scope
- Heavy ceremony loads → consolidate items into a follow-up register

## Anti-patterns

- Plan written once, work goes its own way
- Detailed management plan that the team can't deviate from
- Risks/issues/changes scattered across email and chat
- Items captured but no custodian, no follow-up
- Daily stand-up reduced to status-only with no risk-surfacing

## Cross-refs

- Methodology: [P3.express](../methodologies/p3-express.md) (follow-up register, custodians)
- PRINCE2 §4 [Manage by stages](../principles/prince2/04-manage-by-stages.md)
- Performance domain: [Measurement](06-measurement.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/05-project-work.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/05-project-work.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/performance-domains/06-measurement.md -->
<!-- title: Measurement (performance domain) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Measurement — performance domain

**TL;DR:** Measure to direct work and to evaluate it. Use **forecast
to completion** as the headline metric. **What you measure becomes
the goal** — choose proxies that align with real value.

## Recovering from deviations

- Methodologies use **thresholds** to decide *who* recovers from a
  deviation, not *whether* recovery is needed.
    - Below threshold → team decides
    - Medium → PM decides
    - Above → escalate to higher level
- Common error: thinking "below threshold = no recovery needed".
  Untreated small deviations pile up and become hard to fix.

## Correction vs prevention

- **Corrective action** — fix the deviation now.
- **Preventive action** — fix the root cause so it can't recur.
- Most teams correct and skip prevention → endless firefighting.
- If forced to drop one, **drop correction, keep prevention**.
- Make this explicit in the methodology if it isn't.

## Targets

- Frequent **justification check** is essential. If your method
  lacks one, add it.
- Classical targets: scope, time, cost, quality. Sometimes risk,
  benefits.
- Tune frequency/depth of these checks to *your* project sensitivity.

## Proper measurements

- **What you measure becomes the goal.** Bad proxies create bad
  behavior:
    - Lines of code → bloated code; the great programmer with
      *negative* LOC after cleanup is the cautionary tale.
    - "Items completed per iteration" → conservative under-planning
      (Parkinson's law: work expands to fill the time available).
- Align metrics to real organizational/project goals.
- Direct value measurement (e.g. strategic alignment) is hard;
  fall back to project-target proxies, then refine in portfolio
  management.
- **Forecast-to-completion is the only effective measurement.**
  Example: how much money to finish the project, given current data.

## Reporting

- Audiences differ → multiple report types.
- Verify the message lands; iterate the report if it doesn't.
- People are busy and easily bored → short focused reports.
- If a stakeholder demands long detailed reports for comfort,
  attach a short focused version too.
- Boards / dashboards / *information radiators* are options.
  Don't assume people will visit a dashboard — push a
  notification or short summary.

## When this domain matters more

- Performance-bonus or contract structures with bad-proxy risk
- Long projects with drift between plan and reality
- Multi-stakeholder reporting demands

## Anti-patterns

- Correcting symptoms without preventing root cause
- Threshold = "ignore" rather than "delegate"
- LOC / story-point velocity as primary measure
- Single report style for all audiences
- Long detailed reports as the only output
- Dashboards with no push, no notifications

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §4 [Focus on value](../principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)
- PMBOK 7 §8 [Build quality in](../principles/pmbok-7/08-quality.md)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](05-project-work.md), [Planning](04-planning.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/06-measurement.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/06-measurement.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/performance-domains/07-delivery.md -->
<!-- title: Delivery (performance domain) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Delivery — performance domain

**TL;DR:** **Requirements → Deliverables → Activities** is the
correct order in both predictive and adaptive projects. Limit WIP.
Verify each deliverable before delivery; never bet on a
single big-bang final delivery.

## Start with requirements

- Don't jump to the obvious solution. The "elevator is too slow"
  request was solved by adding a mirror — the real requirement was
  *prevent boredom*, not *increase speed*.
- Process: **Requirements → Deliverables → Activities.**
    - Predictive: most requirements identified up-front.
    - Adaptive: requirements identified gradually.
- When a stakeholder requests a *deliverable*, drill into the
  underlying *requirement*; consider alternative solutions.

### User stories (XP origin, common in Agile)

> As a [role], I want [action] so that [reason].

The "so that" reason works as a requirement and surfaces
constraints. Example: "log in as any user without their password"
becomes safer when the *why* (support investigation) is explicit —
"don't allow logging in as high-privilege admins" follows.

If the methodology is thin on requirements analysis, add it
proportionate to project size/complexity.

## Deliverable breakdowns

- Flat lists fail past trivial size.
- Hierarchical breakdowns (named differently per method):
    - **WBS** — *Work Breakdown Structure* (many)
    - **PBS** — *Product Breakdown Structure* (PRINCE2)
    - **Deliverables map** (P3.express)
- Micro-projects can stay flat (micro.P3.express). Larger projects
  benefit hugely from hierarchy. Most Agile methods lack one — add
  one for larger projects.

## When to deliver

- **Predictive:** mostly one final delivery. Some have stage-end
  deliveries when phases produce usable subsets.
- **Adaptive:** continuous delivery of usable **increments** that
  collect feedback. If full release isn't possible, give increments
  to representative users.
- **Increment definition.** A *usable* set of deliverables. All
  increments are deliverable sets, but not all deliverable sets
  are increments. **Adaptive needs incremental delivery** — without
  it, you can't generate useful feedback.

## Delivery risk

Single big-bang delivery is risky in any approach. Even predictive
projects should review deliverables with the customer/end users for
formal or informal approval throughout the project. Adjust the
methodology if it doesn't enforce this.

## Limit work in progress

- From Lean → strongly adopted in Agile, but applies to all projects.
- Optimal parallelism is usually much lower than people assume.
- Spread thin → easy to abandon hard items and start new ones; fix
  it now is usually cheaper than fixing it later.
- **"Finish before starting more."** Encourage this in your
  methodology — combine with informal acceptance gates from PM and
  customer.

## Quality of work (PM verification)

- PM checks completed deliverables for scope and quality
  alignment to requirements.
- PM doesn't need to be the technical expert — they verify against
  *previously defined scope and quality*.
- Defining scope/quality up-front (or just-in-time per
  deliverable):
    - **Product description** (PRINCE2)
    - Comments on the **deliverables map** (P3.express)
    - **User story acceptance criteria** + **Definition of Done**
      (Agile/Scrum)
- Tailor: adjust detail/formality, separate or merge artifacts.

## When this domain matters more

- Long projects without intermediate user contact
- Software products with high uncertainty about user reaction
- Heavy parallel-work cultures (lots of in-flight items)

## Anti-patterns

- Plans starting from activities or deliverables, skipping
  requirements
- Big-bang delivery at end, no intermediate verification
- WBS as a flat list of tasks
- "WIP limits don't apply, we're predictive"
- PM-as-technical-reviewer (instead of verifying against
  pre-agreed criteria)

## Cross-refs

- PRINCE2 §6 [Focus on products](../principles/prince2/06-focus-on-products.md)
- PMBOK 7 §11 [Adaptability](../principles/pmbok-7/11-adaptability.md)
- PMBOK 7 §12 [Enable change](../principles/pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](05-project-work.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/07-delivery.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/07-delivery.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/performance-domains/08-uncertainty.md -->
<!-- title: Uncertainty (performance domain) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Uncertainty — performance domain

**TL;DR:** Risk management is the dominant topic. Choose the
sophistication that matches the project; default to simpler.
Sensitive risks belong at the **portfolio** level, not project.

## Sophistication ladder (pick the lowest that suffices)

1. **Single combined register (P3.express *follow-up register*).**
   Risks + issues + change requests + improvement plans + lessons
   learned in one place.
   - Reasons:
     - one process covers all of them
     - items morph: a risk that occurs becomes an issue, then a
       lesson learned when closed
2. **Separate risk register (PRINCE2, many).** Spreadsheet with
   risk analysis and planned responses.
3. **Two tables — risks ↔ responses with many-to-many.** A response
   may cover several risks; a risk may need several responses.
4. **Cause / effect split.** A cause produces multiple effects;
   risks have multiple causes.
5. **Graph model.** Move from tables to a graph for the most
   complex / mega-projects.

**Default:** when in doubt, pick the simpler one.

## Quantitative analysis

- Responses must be cost-justified. Insurance €1k/month vs
  millions-of-euro damage is justifiable; vs €2k damage, not.
- Most projects can judge justification intuitively or ad-hoc.
- For sensitive projects, use **Monte Carlo** to compute
  probabilistic outputs before/after responses.
    - Example: P(finish ≤ 22 months) is 85% baseline; spending €80k
      on responses raises it to 98%. Decide if worth it.
- Quantitative analysis is resource-heavy — confirm need before
  adopting.

## Higher management levels

- **Some risks span multiple projects.** They should be managed
  centrally, not duplicated in every project.
- Portfolio management should:
    - maintain an org-wide risk list with responses, available to
      all projects
    - regularly review project-level risks to spot cross-project
      ones and lift them to the portfolio layer
- If your org has no portfolio management:
    - The book is blunt: *implement portfolio management.* No
      shortcut.
    - Practical workaround until then: PMs across projects meet
      monthly to share lessons and high-level risks.
- Distinction: project management = *doing things right*;
  portfolio management = *doing the right things*.

## When this domain matters more

- High-uncertainty, high-impact projects
- Multi-project orgs with cross-cutting risk exposure
- Regulatory/safety-critical contexts (health, aerospace,
  construction)

## Anti-patterns

- Multiple separate registers in a small project (over-engineered)
- Single combined register in a mega-project (under-engineered)
- Monte Carlo simulations on routine projects (waste)
- Each project rediscovering the same org-wide risks
- Risk register set up at kickoff, never reviewed
- Continuing without portfolio management indefinitely

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §9 [Optimize risk responses](../principles/pmbok-7/09-risk.md)
- PMBOK 7 §5 [System interactions](../principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md)
- NUPP §3 [Always be proactive](../principles/nupp/03-always-be-proactive.md)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](05-project-work.md) (follow-up register)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/04-…/08-uncertainty.md`](../../sources/underneath-the-surface/04-enriching-the-methodology/08-uncertainty.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/index.md -->
<!-- title: NUPP principles (distilled) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# NUPP principles — distilled

**NUPP** = *Nearly Universal Principles of Projects*. Open
non-proprietary resource (<https://nupp.guide>) — usable as a lens to
re-interpret any other PM system. Six principles, intentionally
generic and short.

| # | Principle | One-liner |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | [Results over affiliations](01-results-over-affiliations.md) | Don't be loyal to a school — use whatever method actually works. |
| 2 | [Preserve energy and resources](02-preserve-energy.md) | Resources, including your decision-making energy, are finite — apply 80/20. |
| 3 | [Always be proactive](03-always-be-proactive.md) | Reactive default is biological; project work needs proactive action (e.g. risk management). |
| 4 | [Weakest link](04-weakest-link.md) | All domains interact; over-investing in one (e.g. scheduling) wastes the rest. |
| 5 | [Clear purpose](05-clear-purpose.md) | Never adopt a practice without a tangible purpose; otherwise it's cargo-cult. |
| 6 | [Repeatable elements](06-repeatable-elements.md) | Ad-hoc work is expensive; build checklists and cycles to free up cognition. |


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/01-results-over-affiliations.md -->
<!-- title: Prefer results and the truth to affiliations -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Prefer results and the truth to affiliations

**TL;DR:** Don't be tribal about a methodology. Use what works for the
project; learn from systems you don't even use.

## Core ideas

- **Group identity distorts choices.** Loyalty to a method
  ("I'm a Scrum person", "I'm a PRINCE2 expert") biases evaluation
  away from results.
- **Be a polyglot of practices.** Learn multiple systems; pick the
  most appropriate per project.
- **Cross-pollinate.** Even systems you don't adopt can inspire
  improvements to the system you do use.

## When this applies

- Choosing a methodology for a new project
- Resolving methodology debates in an organization
- Designing a tailored system that draws from multiple sources
  (cf. holistic tailoring, not Frankenstein cherry-picking)

## Anti-patterns

- "We're an Agile shop, we don't use anything from PRINCE2"
- Discrediting a method without reading it
- Reducing professional identity to a single framework

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)
- [Methodologies overview](../../methodologies/index.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/01-prefer-results-and-the-truth-to-affiliations.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/01-prefer-results-and-the-truth-to-affiliations.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/02-preserve-energy.md -->
<!-- title: Preserve and optimize energy and resources -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Preserve and optimize energy and resources

**TL;DR:** Project resources *and* the PM's decision-making energy
are both finite. Optimize for high-leverage work and protect mental
bandwidth.

## Core ideas

- **80/20 (Pareto).** Aim for 80% of the benefits at 20% of the
  effort, instead of chasing 100% at 100%.
- **Decision fatigue is real.** Energy spent micromanaging is energy
  not available for true PM responsibilities. Delegate routine
  decisions; reserve mental capacity for the high-impact ones.
- **Same logic applies team-wide.** Help team members preserve their
  energy too — minimize ceremony cost and unnecessary involvement.

## When this applies

- Tailoring decisions: cut activities whose marginal benefit is low
- Choosing whether to micromanage vs. delegate
- Designing meeting cadence and review depth
- Saying "no" to elaborate processes that won't return their cost

## Anti-patterns

- Pursuing 100% completeness in every PM artifact
- PM as decision bottleneck for routine matters
- Bloated ceremonies "for the sake of the methodology"

## Cross-refs

- NUPP §6 [Repeatable elements](06-repeatable-elements.md)
- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/02-preserve-and-optimize-energy-and-resources.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/02-preserve-and-optimize-energy-and-resources.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/03-always-be-proactive.md -->
<!-- title: Always be proactive -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Always be proactive

**TL;DR:** Reactive behavior is the default. Project work consistently
benefits from proactive action — reach out before problems escalate.

## Core ideas

- **Reactive default is biological.** It saves energy and is rational
  in domains where you have no leverage. Projects are different —
  you have leverage.
- **Risk management is the canonical example.** Identify and respond
  to uncertainties *before* they materialize.
- Other domains: stakeholder engagement, quality (prevention vs.
  inspection), proactive communication, scope clarification.

## When this applies

- Setting up risk management practices
- Quality planning (prevention over inspection)
- Stakeholder communications (don't wait for complaints)
- Scope clarification before work starts

## Anti-patterns

- Hero-mode firefighting culture
- Risk register only updated after an incident
- "Let's wait and see" on early-warning signals

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §9 [Optimize risk responses](../pmbok-7/09-risk.md)
- PMBOK 7 §8 [Build quality in](../pmbok-7/08-quality.md)
- PMBOK 7 §3 [Engage stakeholders](../pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md)
- Performance domain: [Uncertainty](../../performance-domains/08-uncertainty.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/03-always-be-proactive.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/03-always-be-proactive.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/04-weakest-link.md -->
<!-- title: A chain is only as strong as its weakest link -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Remember that a chain is only as strong as its weakest link

**TL;DR:** All PM domains interact. Over-investing in one
(typically scheduling) while neglecting others ruins the lot.

## Core ideas

- **Holistic balance.** Time, cost, scope, quality, risk,
  stakeholders, communication — all need adequate attention.
- **Common failure mode.** PMs often over-invest in scheduling while
  under-investing in other domains, which ironically makes the
  schedule itself useless.
- **Diagnostic question:** "Which area of this project is currently
  receiving the least attention?" That is the weak link to address.

## When this applies

- Reviewing where your effort is going as PM
- Designing the PM system: ensure every domain has at least basic coverage
- Audit / health check of an in-flight project

## Anti-patterns

- Lavish Gantt charts; weak risk management
- Detailed scope; nonexistent stakeholder communication
- Strong technical quality; no business case re-checking

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §5 [System interactions](../pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md) (holistic)
- All performance domains in [Enriching the methodology](../../performance-domains/index.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/04-remember-that-a-chain-is-only-as-strong-as-its-weakest-link.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/04-remember-that-a-chain-is-only-as-strong-as-its-weakest-link.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/05-clear-purpose.md -->
<!-- title: Don't do anything without a clear purpose -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Don't do anything without a clear purpose

**TL;DR:** Every practice/artifact must have a purpose you can name.
Otherwise it's cargo-cult work — looks like methodology, produces
nothing.

## Core ideas

- **Counterfactual test.** Imagine two worlds, identical except for
  this practice/artifact. Is the difference worth the effort?
  If you can't articulate the difference, skip the practice.
- **Without purpose, two failure modes:**
    1. The practice has no benefit *in your context*; you waste effort.
    2. The practice has potential benefit, but you do it
       mechanically and miss the benefit.
- **Templates are dangerous tools.** A PRINCE2 *highlight report*
  template, filled in mechanically, ≠ an effective highlight
  report. Read the methodology, understand the purpose, build a
  document that *serves* the purpose, then compare to the template
  and reconcile differences only when you understand them.

## When this applies

- Adopting any new artifact/ceremony from a methodology
- Deciding whether to keep an existing practice that nobody likes
- Building tailored templates from scratch instead of copy-pasting
- Code reviews of a methodology rollout

## Anti-patterns

- Downloading a template, filling it, sending it on schedule
- Adopting Scrum/PRINCE2/etc. ceremonies without articulating why
- Compliance theater: doing it because the standard says so

## Cross-refs

- Cargo Cult Science (Feynman) — referenced in [Ch. 2 overview](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/index.md)
- NUPP §2 [Preserve energy](02-preserve-energy.md)
- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/05-don-t-do-anything-without-a-clear-purpose.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/05-don-t-do-anything-without-a-clear-purpose.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/nupp/06-repeatable-elements.md -->
<!-- title: Use repeatable elements -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Use repeatable elements

**TL;DR:** Ad-hoc work is expensive and error-prone. Build checklists
and recurring cycles so cognition can go to the actual work.

## Core ideas

- **Cost of ad-hoc.** Re-deciding routine matters every time wastes
  energy and risks missing items.
- **Checklists.** For frequently-checked items, build a checklist;
  it offloads memory and surfaces improvement ideas as you use it
  (continuous improvement).
- **Repeatable cycles.** Methodology cadences (e.g. P3.express
  monthly cycle) become second nature, freeing cognition for the
  unique parts of the project.

## When this applies

- Quality checks, risk reviews, status reports
- Onboarding/offboarding routines
- Methodology rhythm design (daily/weekly/monthly cycles)

## Anti-patterns

- Hand-crafting every status update from scratch
- Ad-hoc quality checks that miss steps under pressure
- Reinventing the wheel for routine ceremonies

## Cross-refs

- Methodology: [P3.express](../../methodologies/p3-express.md) (monthly cycles)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](../../performance-domains/05-project-work.md)
- NUPP §2 [Preserve energy](02-preserve-energy.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/06-use-repeatable-elements.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/02-nupp-principles/06-use-repeatable-elements.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/index.md -->
<!-- title: PMBOK 7 principles (distilled) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# PMBOK 7 principles — distilled

Twelve principles that PMBOK 7 uses as a **lens to interpret** project
management situations. They are **not** a methodology and **not** a
checklist of activities — apply them to evaluate decisions and tailor
methods.

| # | Principle | One-liner |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | [Stewardship](01-stewardship.md) | The PM is a facilitator and steward, not a technical boss. |
| 2 | [Collaborative team](02-collaborative-team.md) | Build a team, not a collection of stars; embed team-building in real work. |
| 3 | [Engage stakeholders](03-engage-stakeholders.md) | Identify everyone who can impact the project; communicate frequently and tailored. |
| 4 | [Focus on value](04-focus-on-value.md) | Value = benefits ÷ cost; align every decision with the project's "why". |
| 5 | [System interactions](05-system-interactions.md) | Decisions ripple; evaluate consequences across the whole system, not in isolation. |
| 6 | [Leadership](06-leadership.md) | Leadership is a behavior, not a role; anyone can lead in their domain. |
| 7 | [Tailor based on context](07-tailor.md) | No universal method; adapt to project, organization, environment. |
| 8 | [Build quality in](08-quality.md) | Quality is built into processes and deliverables, not inspected at the end. |
| 9 | [Optimize risk responses](09-risk.md) | Spend on risk responses only when their cost is justified by the risk reduction. |
| 10 | [Navigate complexity](10-complexity.md) | Recognize complexity sources; respond with simplification and adaptation. |
| 11 | [Adaptability and resiliency](11-adaptability.md) | Build the capacity to handle and recover from change. |
| 12 | [Enable change](12-enable-change.md) | Project outputs only create value when stakeholders adopt them; manage adoption. |


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/01-stewardship.md -->
<!-- title: Stewardship -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Stewardship — *Be a diligent, respectful, and caring steward*

**TL;DR:** A project manager is a facilitator and steward of people
and resources, not a technical decision-maker or a "boss".

## Core ideas

- **Manager ≠ promoted expert.** Becoming a manager is a *career
  change*, not a *promotion*. Managerial skill is independent of
  technical skill; promoting senior experts into PM roles loses a
  good expert and gains a mediocre manager.
- **PM as chief facilitator.** A PM coordinates, removes obstacles,
  resolves conflict, and creates a safe productive environment so
  experts can do their work. Renaming the role to "chief project
  facilitator" can correct culture if "PM" carries baggage.
- **Technical knowledge is optional, possibly counter-productive.**
  Three positions exist; the author favors the strongest: a PM
  *without* technical knowledge is less likely to micromanage. If
  you do have technical expertise, **draw a hard line** between
  technical and managerial decisions and stay out of the former.
- **Stewardship covers people and resources.** Care, integrity,
  trustworthiness, compliance with rules and ethics.
- **Listen to understand, not to respond.** Initiate conversation;
  hunt for problems early.

## When this applies

- Selecting or promoting project managers
- Defining the PM job description and competency framework
- Coaching new PMs out of "technical boss" habits
- Choosing whether to staff a PM with or without domain expertise

## Anti-patterns

- Promoting the most senior technical expert "as a reward"
- A PM who micromanages technical decisions because they "know better"
- PM-as-status-symbol with no service mindset
- Waiting for issues to surface instead of seeking them out

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §6 [Demonstrate leadership behaviors](06-leadership.md)
- PMBOK 7 §2 [Collaborative team environment](02-collaborative-team.md)
- Performance domain: [Team](../../performance-domains/02-team.md)
- External: PMI Code of Ethics; P3.express Code of Conduct; IPMA ICB

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/01-be-a-diligent-respectful-and-caring-steward.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/01-be-a-diligent-respectful-and-caring-steward.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/02-collaborative-team.md -->
<!-- title: Collaborative team environment -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Collaborative team environment

**TL;DR:** A great project needs a great *team*, not just great
individuals. Build the team through real work, not corporate rituals.

## Core ideas

- **Team-building is embedded, not separate.** Replace lone planning
  with facilitated workshops. Use any work activity as a chance to
  improve relationships and shared understanding.
- **Use methodology hooks for collaboration.** P3.express monthly
  closing has stakeholder-satisfaction evaluation + group
  improvement-planning (e.g. via Delphi). Group involvement creates
  buy-in and surfaces ideas a single PM would miss.
- **Common understanding requires explicit structure.** For projects
  past trivial size:
    - clarify what each person does and what they can expect from
      others (PRINCE2 "defined roles & responsibilities")
    - formalize work flow (who reviews what, in how many days)
    - agree explicit *ground rules* of behavior
- **Facilitate the team to set rules, do not dictate.** Top-down
  rules feel bossy and get rejected; team-set rules are realistic
  and adopted. Example: P3.express Code of Conduct was crowd-sourced.

## When this applies

- Kicking off a new project team
- Planning workshops, retrospectives, decision workshops
- Setting up review/approval flows in a methodology
- Drafting a project charter or team agreement

## Anti-patterns

- "Team-building" trips/games disconnected from real work
- PM writes rules, hands them down, expects compliance
- Treating planning as a one-person task to be efficient
- No agreed flow → constant rework and unclear handoffs

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §1 [Stewardship](01-stewardship.md) — facilitating ≠ bossing
- PRINCE2 principle: [Defined roles and responsibilities](../prince2/03-roles.md)
- Performance domain: [Team](../../performance-domains/02-team.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/02-create-a-collaborative-project-team-environment.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/02-create-a-collaborative-project-team-environment.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md -->
<!-- title: Engage stakeholders -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Effectively engage with stakeholders

**TL;DR:** A stakeholder is *anyone* with interest who can impact
the project (not just the customer). Identify them aggressively,
communicate often, and tailor the channel per group.

## Core ideas

- **Definition matters.** Stakeholder = any person/group with
  interest in the project who can impact it: team, client,
  suppliers, end users, regulators, competitors, the public.
- **Identification is hard, do it with the team.** Group
  identification surfaces stakeholders a PM working alone misses;
  doubles as team-building.
- **Communicate frequently and proactively.** Different audiences
  need different channels (online dashboards, on-site visits,
  in-person meetings, written reports). "Working well is not
  enough; let others know how well you work."
- **Hostile stakeholders can become champions.** Approach with
  curiosity (ask them to list everything that could go wrong, take
  notes without rebutting). Return with responses to their concerns
  and ask for feedback. Power-holders converted this way bring
  resources and political support.
- **Engagement = decision power.** Delegate decisions within bounds.
  PRINCE2 *manage by exception*: each level acts within thresholds
  on time/cost/quality/risk/benefit; escalate only when exceeded.
- **Use methodology rhythms.** P3.express monthly stakeholder
  satisfaction evaluation + *focused communication* (predefined
  audience-specific messages at process milestones).

## When this applies

- Project initiation: stakeholder register
- Conflict, scope changes, disgruntled groups
- Setting up communication plans and reporting cadences
- Defining decision-authority thresholds (manage by exception)

## Anti-patterns

- Treating "stakeholder" as a synonym for "customer"
- One channel for everyone (e.g. email-only for non-tech audiences)
- Ignoring negative stakeholders or labeling them "always negative"
- PM as bottleneck for every decision (no delegation)
- Reactive comms: waiting until stakeholders complain

## Cross-refs

- Performance domain: [Stakeholders](../../performance-domains/01-stakeholders.md)
- PRINCE2 [manage by exception](../prince2/05-manage-by-exception.md)
- Methodology: [P3.express](../../methodologies/p3-express.md) (monthly satisfaction eval)
- NUPP principle: [Don't do anything without a clear purpose](../nupp/05-clear-purpose.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/03-effectively-engage-with-stakeholders.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/03-effectively-engage-with-stakeholders.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md -->
<!-- title: Focus on value -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Focus on value

**TL;DR:** Value is the **benefits-to-cost ratio** (not "benefits"
alone). Every project decision must be traceable to *why* the
project exists. Value lives at the portfolio layer; projects align
to it and feed it information.

## Core ideas

- **Decisions need the "why".** A trade-off between time/quality/cost
  cannot be answered by the project team in isolation; it depends on
  whether the project's purpose is, for example, market entry
  (preserve quality, spend more) or revenue (cut quality if needed).
  Escalate such decisions to the level that owns the why.
- **Justification ≠ Value.** PRINCE2 frames the "why" as
  *business case justification*, continually re-checked. PMBOK 7
  uses *value*, with justification nested inside.
- **Definition: value = benefits ÷ cost.** A €1,000-benefit project
  costing €100 is twice as valuable as one costing €200. Beware:
  "value" in everyday speech often means "benefit"; mixing the two
  causes bad decisions.
- **Benefits are subjective and broader than money.** Lives saved,
  environmental impact, reputation, market entry, capability
  building. Each organization has its own *value drivers*.
- **Value is holistic and cross-temporal.** A project's value
  depends on adjacent projects, organizational strategy, regulation,
  even future products. Outputs can amplify each other (e.g. one
  product launch lifts sales of others).
- **Short vs long term depends on context.** Same investment, lump
  benefit now vs annuity-style return for 20 years — choice depends
  on cash position and existing portfolio mix.
- **Value is owned by portfolio management.** That layer selects and
  prioritizes projects. Project layer must (a) align activities to
  value drivers and (b) provide up-to-date data so portfolio can
  re-evaluate.

## When this applies

- Major scope/quality/schedule trade-offs (escalate to owner of why)
- Drafting/refreshing the business case
- Portfolio prioritization and project selection
- Reporting up: surface data that lets portfolio re-evaluate
- Choosing between short-term and long-term opportunities

## Anti-patterns

- Treating "value" as synonymous with "benefit"
- Project-local optimization that ignores org/portfolio context
- Static business case that is never re-checked after kickoff
- PM unilaterally trades quality for time without escalation
- Value drivers undefined → every stakeholder optimizes their own

## Cross-refs

- PRINCE2 principle: [Continued business justification](../prince2/01-continued-business-justification.md)
- Performance domain: [Delivery](../../performance-domains/07-delivery.md)
- NUPP principle: [Don't do anything without a clear purpose](../nupp/05-clear-purpose.md)
- Concept: portfolio management (see [Glossary](../../glossary.md#portfolio-management))

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/04-focus-on-value.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/04-focus-on-value.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md -->
<!-- title: System interactions -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Recognize, evaluate, and respond to system interactions

**TL;DR:** Decisions ripple through interconnected systems and are
distorted by cognitive biases. Reason holistically and apply
critical thinking, not gut feel.

## Core ideas

- **Systems thinking = critical thinking + holistic thinking.**
  Reason about non-linear interactions; the project's outcome
  depends on org context, other projects, future plans, regulation.
- **Use expected-value reasoning across many decisions.** A single
  trial doesn't matter — the strategy across thousands of similar
  decisions does. Pick the higher EV consistently.
- **EV is conditional on risk appetite.** A high-EV bet is only
  rational if the downside fits within the loss you can bear.
  Money has non-linear utility: losing your last €200 is worse
  than losing one of many €200s.
- **Cognitive biases distort decisions.** Notable ones in projects:
    - **Loss aversion** — we weight losses ~2.5× gains; refuse
      good bets out of fear
    - **Confirmation bias** — seeking only data that supports a hypothesis
    - **Sunk cost fallacy** — continuing because of past investment
    - **Effort justification** — overvaluing what we worked hard on
    - **Planning fallacy** — systematic underestimation of duration/cost
    - **Halo effect**, **illusion of control**, **base rate fallacy**,
      **zero risk bias**, **stereotyping**
- **You can manage biases consciously.** Learn them; build review
  steps and decision frameworks that surface them.

## When this applies

- Major go/no-go decisions with significant downside
- Risk response design (calibration of EV vs. risk appetite)
- Budget/schedule estimation (planning fallacy)
- Pivot/cancel decisions (sunk cost, effort justification)
- Reviewing forecasts that "feel right" without explicit reasoning

## Anti-patterns

- "Gut feel" decisions on high-impact issues
- Refusing positive-EV bets because "we might lose"
- Doubling down on a failing project because of past spend
- Ignoring base rates ("our project will be different")
- Local optimization that ignores org/portfolio context

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §4 [Focus on value](04-focus-on-value.md) (holistic value)
- PMBOK 7 §10 [Navigate complexity](10-complexity.md)
- Performance domain: [Uncertainty](../../performance-domains/08-uncertainty.md)
- External: Wikipedia "List of cognitive biases"

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/05-recognize-evaluate-and-respond-to-system-interactions.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/05-recognize-evaluate-and-respond-to-system-interactions.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/06-leadership.md -->
<!-- title: Leadership behaviors -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Demonstrate leadership behaviors

**TL;DR:** Leadership is influencing others toward desired outcomes —
a *behavior*, not a job title. Anyone can lead; PMs should both
*be* leaders and *find and empower* hidden leaders.

## Core ideas

- **Leadership ≠ management role.** Anyone who influences people
  toward outcomes is a leader, regardless of position. Some of the
  highest-impact people in an organization hold non-leadership
  titles (e.g. a long-tenured admin who informally coordinates
  everything).
- **Identify and support hidden leaders.** They self-actualize
  through the work; small denials (no raise, no recognition) push
  them out and the loss only becomes visible afterwards.
- **Motivation is contextual.** A monetary bonus motivates only when
  the amount-to-need ratio is high enough. A small cash amount can
  *demotivate*; a symbolic gift (e.g. engraved pen + sincere thanks)
  on the same budget often outperforms it.
- **Charisma is plural.** Charisma forms include knowledge,
  kindness, supportiveness, calm confidence — not only
  movie-style toughness. Find your natural form and develop it.
- **Skill set (per APMBoK):**
    - Communication
    - Conflict management
    - Delegation
    - Influencing
    - Leadership
    - Negotiation
    - Teamwork

## When this applies

- Recognizing and rewarding non-managerial contributors
- Designing a recognition / bonus / appreciation programme
- Choosing how to deliver praise or hard feedback
- Personal development planning for PMs
- Retention conversations (don't lose hidden leaders cheaply)

## Anti-patterns

- Treating "leadership" as synonymous with "manager" or "PM"
- Trying to imitate a single charisma stereotype that fits poorly
- Low-amount cash bonuses that imply the contribution is cheap
- Letting key informal leaders leave over a denied small raise

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §1 [Stewardship](01-stewardship.md) (PM as facilitator)
- PMBOK 7 §2 [Collaborative team](02-collaborative-team.md)
- Performance domain: [Team](../../performance-domains/02-team.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/06-demonstrate-leadership-behaviors.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/06-demonstrate-leadership-behaviors.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/07-tailor.md -->
<!-- title: Tailor based on context -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Tailor based on context

**TL;DR:** No universal PM system fits every project. Tailor a
chosen methodology to your size/culture/project — but tailoring is
system design, not cherry-picking.

## Core ideas

- **Match the system to the environment.** Copying another
  organization's mature system into a smaller/different company
  fails. The transplanted system was customized for a specific
  size, culture, history; reproducing it as-is breaks.
- **Tailor instead of rejecting.** "PRINCE2 is too expensive for us"
  usually means *un-tailored* PRINCE2. Properly tailored, even
  heavyweight methods can save cost.
- **Tailoring example.** PRINCE2 *checkpoint reports* are a
  *management product*, not a document format. In a small project
  this can be a phone call at fixed intervals. The decision about
  channel/depth is the act of tailoring.
- **Two-step tailoring (PMBOK 7).**
    1. **Organization-level** partial tailoring covering elements
       common to all projects.
    2. **Project-level** final tailoring for that project's
       specifics.
- **Cherry-picking ≠ tailoring.** Mixing fragments from different
  systems without internal consistency yields a "Frankenstein"
  system. Tailoring is holistic system design.
- **Tailoring is its own skill.** Most methods leave it to the PM,
  but few PMs are also system designers. DSDM addresses this with
  a dedicated **DSDM Coach** role.
- **When to tailor depends on the method:**
    - **Maximalist** (PRINCE2): tailor *upfront* — the system is too
      heavy to use untailored.
    - **Minimalist** (P3.express): use as-is, tailor *gradually* by
      enriching after experience.
- **Keep upfront tailoring minimum.** It's easy to design a fancy
  system that proves heavy in practice. Add elements as real needs
  surface.

## When this applies

- Organization rolling out a PM standard
- New project starting under an existing methodology
- A team complaining "the methodology doesn't fit"
- Choosing between a maximalist and minimalist starting point
- Defining a PM competency framework (include system-design skills)

## Anti-patterns

- Cherry-picking practices from multiple frameworks
- Using PRINCE2/PMBOK "by the book" without tailoring
- Heavy upfront tailoring → unused, ignored, or resented
- Assuming any senior PM can tailor a methodology
- "We're too small for X" instead of asking "how would X scale to us?"

## Cross-refs

- PRINCE2 principle: [Tailor to suit the project](../prince2/07-tailor-to-project.md)
- Methodology choice: [Methodologies](../../methodologies/index.md) — maximalist vs minimalist
- Performance domain: [Development approach and life cycle](../../performance-domains/03-development-approach.md)
- DSDM Coach role: [DSDM](../../methodologies/dsdm.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/07-tailor-based-on-context.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/07-tailor-based-on-context.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/08-quality.md -->
<!-- title: Build quality in -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Build quality into processes and deliverables

**TL;DR:** Quality is *prevention*, integrated into the system, not
final inspection bolted on. It applies to the *product* and to the
*PM system itself*. Investment must be net-positive; if not, the
quality system is poorly tailored.

## Core ideas

- **Net-positive cost-benefit.** "Quality is too expensive" means it
  isn't tailored. Effective quality work reduces rework cost,
  improves reputation, and saves time overall.
- **Prevention over inspection.** Final testing is necessary but
  insufficient. The main goal is to prevent defects, not catch
  them. (This aligns with NUPP "always be proactive".)
- **Integrate, don't bolt on.** Quality as an afterthought is
  ineffective. Build it into every process.
- **Two layers to manage:**
    1. Quality of the **product** and its production processes.
    2. Quality of the **PM system** itself.
- **Product quality is domain-specific.** PM doesn't need to know
  every method, but must ensure they are *identified, documented,
  executed*. Sources of requirements: customer approval, internal
  organization standards, national/regulatory codes.
    - IT example: automated/manual tests + pair programming for
      proactive defect prevention
    - Construction example: destructive and non-destructive concrete
      tests + cement/sand/water mix specs + curing procedures
- **Managerial quality via peer reviews.** P3.express recommends a
  PM peer review at intervals, ideally with a *different* reviewer
  each time to maximize diversity of feedback. Both parties learn.

## When this applies

- Drafting a quality plan
- Pushback that "quality costs too much" (test net cost-benefit)
- Choosing between proactive (e.g. pair programming) and reactive
  (testing only) quality methods
- Building review cadence into the PM's own work, not just the team's

## Anti-patterns

- Quality = final testing only
- Standalone "quality department" disconnected from delivery
- Untailored quality plan more expensive than the savings it brings
- No mechanism to evaluate the *PM's* own work quality
- Same peer reviewer every time → narrow feedback

## Cross-refs

- NUPP principle: [Always be proactive](../nupp/03-always-be-proactive.md)
- PMBOK 7 §9 [Optimize risk responses](09-risk.md) (proactive risk handling)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](../../performance-domains/05-project-work.md)
- Methodology: [P3.express](../../methodologies/p3-express.md) (peer reviews)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/08-build-quality-into-processes-and-deliverables.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/08-build-quality-into-processes-and-deliverables.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/09-risk.md -->
<!-- title: Optimize risk responses -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Optimize risk responses

**TL;DR:** A *risk* is any uncertainty (positive or negative) that
can impact the project. Manage risks proactively through layered
responses, continuously, with cost-justified investments.

## Core ideas

- **Definition.** Risk = an uncertainty that can affect the project,
  *either way*. Distinct from certainties (managed by the plan).
- **Identification is continuous, not one-shot.** Start with
  workshops at kickoff, then keep looking. Practice: a short fixed
  agenda item at the end of every meeting to surface new risks.
  Risks emerge as the project progresses and the environment shifts.
- **Layered response design** (concrete safety example):
    1. Add an activity to the plan that prevents the risk
       (build temporary handrails immediately when a floor is laid)
    2. Adjust the plan to reduce exposure
       (limit work on unfinished floors until permanent walls)
    3. Capacity-building responses that cover *several* risks at
       once (health-and-safety training)
    4. Reduce *impact* of residual risk if it occurs
       (on-site nurse / first aid)
    5. Transfer/finance residual risk
       (insurance policy)
- **Investment must be justified.** Pay for risk responses only when
  the response cost is justified by the expected reduction in risk.
  (See system-interactions principle on EV reasoning.)
- **Why this matters.** Proper risk management reduces cost,
  protects reputation, fulfills social responsibility, and improves
  project predictability.

## When this applies

- Project initiation: initial risk register
- Recurring meeting cadence (add risk-scan to closing 5 minutes)
- Designing safety plans / quality plans / contingency plans
- Deciding whether to buy insurance and at what coverage
- Evaluating mitigations: pick highest-coverage-per-cost responses

## Anti-patterns

- One-time risk identification at kickoff, never revisited
- Single-layer responses (only insurance, or only training)
- Treating risk only as negative (missing positive risks)
- Risk-response cost greater than the risk itself reduces
- "We covered safety in onboarding" without a project-specific plan

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §5 [System interactions](05-system-interactions.md) — EV reasoning
- PMBOK 7 §8 [Build quality in](08-quality.md) — prevention mindset
- NUPP principle: [Always be proactive](../nupp/03-always-be-proactive.md)
- Performance domain: [Uncertainty](../../performance-domains/08-uncertainty.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/09-optimize-risk-responses.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/09-optimize-risk-responses.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/10-complexity.md -->
<!-- title: Navigate complexity -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Navigate complexity

**TL;DR:** Project complexity comes from three sources: people,
non-linear interactions, and ambiguity/uncertainty. There is no
distinct technique here — applying the other principles *is* how you
navigate complexity.

> The author considers this a redundant principle: each contributing
> source already has its own dedicated principle. PMBOK 7 keeps it
> as a separate principle for emphasis.

## Core ideas

- **Three sources of complexity** in projects:
    1. **People** — humans and their interactions create
       complexity (covered by Principles 1–3: stewardship,
       collaborative team, stakeholder engagement).
    2. **Non-linear interactions** — system effects that are not
       additive (covered by Principle 5: system interactions; also
       Principle 4: holistic value).
    3. **Ambiguity and uncertainty** — risks (covered by
       Principle 9: optimize risk responses).
- **No new techniques to introduce here.** Application of
  principles 1, 2, 3, 5, 9 *is* the practice of navigating
  complexity.

## When this applies

- When complexity is rising: don't look for a single new tool;
  re-apply the relevant primary principle (people / system / risk).
- When teaching the principles: use this as a synthesis frame.

## Anti-patterns

- Looking for a magical "complexity tool"
- Treating complexity as a separate workstream rather than a
  property of people, systems, and uncertainty

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §1 [Stewardship](01-stewardship.md)
- PMBOK 7 §2 [Collaborative team](02-collaborative-team.md)
- PMBOK 7 §3 [Engage stakeholders](03-engage-stakeholders.md)
- PMBOK 7 §5 [System interactions](05-system-interactions.md)
- PMBOK 7 §9 [Optimize risk responses](09-risk.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/10-navigate-complexity.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/10-navigate-complexity.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/11-adaptability.md -->
<!-- title: Adaptability and resiliency -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Embrace adaptability and resiliency

**TL;DR:** Predictive vs adaptive (Agile) approaches each fit a
different *kind of product*. PMBOK 7 is approach-agnostic for the
product, but the **PM system itself** must always be adaptive
because it deals with people.

## Core ideas

- **Predictive approach.** Design upfront, then build. Right when
  the output is fully knowable in advance and end-user feedback
  isn't a major source of design (Mars rover, bridge).
- **Adaptive (Agile) approach.** Build subsets, expose them to end
  users, collect feedback, decide next step. Right when output
  value depends on user acceptance (most software applications).
- **Both are valid.** Pick by output type, not by fashion. PMBOK 7
  avoids "waterfall" / "traditional" labels because they carry
  negative connotations.
- **Common myth.** PMBOK 7 has *not* "become Agile". It is
  *approach-agnostic* — supports both predictive and adaptive product
  development equally.
- **Two layers, not one.**
    1. **Product approach** — choose predictive *or* adaptive based
       on the product nature.
    2. **PM system approach** — must be adaptive *regardless* of
       product, because it manages complex human relations. Use a
       base PM system and enrich it gradually; if not, at least
       revise as the project progresses.

## When this applies

- Choosing a development life cycle (predictive / iterative /
  incremental / adaptive / hybrid)
- Defending the choice against fashion-driven pressure ("we must
  go Agile" or "Agile is just chaos")
- Designing the PM system itself: always reserve room for
  adaptation; don't lock it in at kickoff
- Hybrid projects: predictive for some components, adaptive for
  others

## Anti-patterns

- Forcing Agile on a fully predictable, design-once-build-once project
- Forcing Predictive on a high-uncertainty user-driven product
- Calling PMBOK 7 "Agile" or "obsolete because of Agile"
- Locking the PM system upfront and refusing to adapt it

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](07-tailor.md)
- Performance domain: [Development approach and life cycle](../../performance-domains/03-development-approach.md)
- Methodology comparisons: [Methodologies](../../methodologies/index.md)
  (P3.express minimalist + adaptive PM system; PRINCE2 maximalist;
  Scrum/DSDM Agile)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/11-embrace-adaptability-and-resiliency.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/11-embrace-adaptability-and-resiliency.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md -->
<!-- title: Enable change to achieve the envisioned future state -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Enable change to achieve the envisioned future state

**TL;DR:** Distinguish **output** (what the project builds) from
**outcome** (what changes in the world). Outputs only matter when
they cause adoption and outcomes; outcome management is largely
program management, but the project still has to align and report.

## Core ideas

- **Output ≠ Outcome.** Implementing a document-management system is
  the *output*; *easier and more efficient access to documents* is
  the *outcome*. Confusing the two is a common source of project
  failure.
- **Project produces output; outcomes need more.** Outcomes usually
  require additional activities (training, change-management,
  follow-on projects). Therefore outcomes are managed primarily at
  the **program management** layer.
- **Project still has responsibilities for outcomes.**
    1. Understand *why* the project exists and what outcomes it is
       meant to enable.
    2. Align project activities with that intent.
    3. Provide the program/portfolio layer with information they
       need to manage the outcomes.
- **Reframing for the PM:** see yourself as a *change enabler*, not
  a deliverable factory. Successful project = a project whose
  output produces the intended changes in stakeholder behavior and
  organizational state.

## When this applies

- Defining project success criteria (outcome-aware, not just output)
- Drafting a benefits-realization plan
- Stakeholder change-management planning around delivery
- Reporting up to program/portfolio level: include adoption signals
- Choosing whether activities like training/communication belong
  inside the project, in a program, or as a separate project

## Anti-patterns

- Project marked "successful" on output delivery while the outcome
  fails to materialize (no adoption)
- "Throw it over the wall" delivery → no follow-up, no training
- PM optimizes only for output metrics, ignores outcome signals
- Treating the project as standalone without program/portfolio context

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §4 [Focus on value](04-focus-on-value.md) (outcomes drive value)
- Performance domain: [Delivery](../../performance-domains/07-delivery.md)
- Concept: program management (see [Glossary](../../glossary.md#program-management))

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/12-enable-change-to-achieve-the-envisioned-future-state.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/01-pmbok-7-principles/12-enable-change-to-achieve-the-envisioned-future-state.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/index.md -->
<!-- title: PRINCE2 principles (distilled) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# PRINCE2® principles — distilled

Seven principles that **support the PRINCE2 methodology**. Mostly
applicable to all projects, but designed as a coherent set with the
PRINCE2 method (covered separately in [Methodologies](../../methodologies/prince2.md)).

| # | Principle | One-liner |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | [Continued business justification](01-continued-business-justification.md) | Re-check the business case at predefined gates; avoid sunk-cost continuation. |
| 2 | [Learn from experience](02-learn-from-experience.md) | Use existing PM systems and prior project lessons; document and share. |
| 3 | [Defined roles and responsibilities](03-roles.md) | Beyond very small projects, people need to know what's expected and from whom. |
| 4 | [Manage by stages](04-manage-by-stages.md) | High-level plan up-front; detailed plan only for the next stage. |
| 5 | [Manage by exception](05-manage-by-exception.md) | Delegate within tolerances; escalate only when thresholds are breached. |
| 6 | [Focus on products](06-focus-on-products.md) | Plan around what is produced (products), not around activities. |
| 7 | [Tailor to suit the project](07-tailor-to-project.md) | PRINCE2 (and any system) must be tailored, then continuously adapted. |


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/01-continued-business-justification.md -->
<!-- title: Continued business justification -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Continued business justification

**TL;DR:** Re-check the project's business case throughout — at
predefined gates. Things change; sunk cost is not a reason to continue.

## Core ideas

- A project that *was* justifiable can stop being so as conditions
  change.
- Two failure modes: blindness to the change, and **sunk cost
  fallacy** (continuing because of past investment).
- Mitigation: predefined, recurring **gates** where the team
  explicitly asks "should we continue, given the latest data?"
- Even when justification holds, gates re-anchor the team on *why*
  the project exists and keep activities aligned.

## When this applies

- Designing a stage-gate process for a project
- Mid-project pivots, scope shocks, market shifts
- Portfolio review (does this project still belong in the mix?)

## Anti-patterns

- One-time business case at kickoff, never reviewed
- Continuing because "we've already spent so much"
- Treating gate reviews as compliance theater

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §4 [Focus on value](../pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)
- PMBOK 7 §5 [System interactions](../pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md) (sunk cost)
- PRINCE2 §4 [Manage by stages](04-manage-by-stages.md) (gates align with stages)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/01-continued-business-justification.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/01-continued-business-justification.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/02-learn-from-experience.md -->
<!-- title: Learn from experience -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Learn from experience

**TL;DR:** Reusing existing PM systems and prior-project lessons is
cheaper than reinventing them. Both **between** and **within** projects.

## Core ideas

- **Between projects:** adopt established systems (PRINCE2,
  P3.express, PMBOK Guide) instead of building from scratch.
- **Within an organization:** document lessons learned, especially
  from similar projects; make them findable and consultable.
- Requires a working mechanism for capture and sharing — without it,
  the lessons stay tacit and walk out the door with people.

## When this applies

- Project initiation: search prior similar projects
- Closing: capture lessons in a usable, discoverable form
- Designing a PMO knowledge base / lessons repository

## Anti-patterns

- "Every project is unique, lessons don't transfer"
- Lessons captured in ad-hoc docs nobody reads
- Building a methodology from zero ignoring established frameworks

## Cross-refs

- Methodology: [PRINCE2](../../methodologies/prince2.md)
- NUPP §6 [Repeatable elements](../nupp/06-repeatable-elements.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/02-learn-from-experience.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/02-learn-from-experience.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/03-roles.md -->
<!-- title: Defined roles and responsibilities -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Defined roles and responsibilities

**TL;DR:** Beyond very small projects, people need explicit clarity
on what's expected of them — and what they can expect from others.

## Core ideas

- Required as soon as the project is past trivial size.
- Reciprocal expectations: define both what each person *does* and
  what they can *demand* from others.
- A baseline for accountability, escalation, and conflict resolution.

## When this applies

- Project kick-off / charter
- Onboarding new members mid-project
- Recurring confusion or hand-off failures (the diagnostic)

## Anti-patterns

- "We're agile, we don't need roles" applied to a 30-person delivery
- Roles documented but never communicated
- Authority defined; expectations on the role-holder undefined

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §2 [Collaborative team](../pmbok-7/02-collaborative-team.md)
- Performance domain: [Team](../../performance-domains/02-team.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/03-defined-roles-and-responsibilities.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/03-defined-roles-and-responsibilities.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/04-manage-by-stages.md -->
<!-- title: Manage by stages -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Manage by stages

**TL;DR:** You can only plan in detail for a short horizon. Use a
high-level plan up-front and a detailed plan only for the next stage.

## Core ideas

- **Limited foresight.** Detailed planning past a short window is
  speculation that creates false precision.
- **Rolling wave.** Outline plan for the whole project; detailed
  plan only for the *next* stage; refresh before each stage.
- Stages naturally align with **business case re-checks** (gates).

## When this applies

- Long-running or uncertain projects
- Programs with multi-year horizons
- Any project where early estimates would be heavily speculative

## Anti-patterns

- Trying to lock detailed plan to the project's last day at kickoff
- "Big upfront plan" that nobody updates
- No high-level plan, only stage-by-stage tunnel vision

## Cross-refs

- PRINCE2 §1 [Continued business justification](01-continued-business-justification.md) (stage gates)
- Performance domain: [Planning](../../performance-domains/04-planning.md)
- Methodology: [PRINCE2](../../methodologies/prince2.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/04-manage-by-stages.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/04-manage-by-stages.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/05-manage-by-exception.md -->
<!-- title: Manage by exception -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Manage by exception

**TL;DR:** Delegate decisions within tolerances; escalate only when
thresholds are breached. Saves senior energy and creates buy-in.

## Core ideas

- **Tolerances per level.** Each management level has explicit
  thresholds (time, cost, quality, scope, risk, benefit).
- **Within → autonomous.** Below threshold the level decides without
  interference.
- **Above → escalate.** Excursions are *exceptions* that go up.
- **Why it works.** Faster decisions, more decision power for those
  closer to the work, buy-in from the delegates, scarce attention
  reserved for genuine deviations.

## When this applies

- Defining the project board / steering committee charter
- Setting up multi-team or multi-tier delivery
- Reducing PM bottlenecks on routine choices
- Drafting contracts with delegated authority

## Anti-patterns

- No tolerances → every decision escalates
- Tolerances exist but are not communicated
- Steering committee involved in routine matters
- Escalation only allowed retroactively (after a breach)

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §3 [Engage stakeholders](../pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md) (delegation = engagement)
- NUPP §2 [Preserve energy](../nupp/02-preserve-energy.md)
- Methodology: [PRINCE2](../../methodologies/prince2.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/05-manage-by-exception.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/05-manage-by-exception.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/06-focus-on-products.md -->
<!-- title: Focus on products -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Focus on products

**TL;DR:** Plan around the products (deliverables), not around
activities. Activity-first planning prematurely commits to one solution.

## Core ideas

- **Activity bias.** Listing activities first locks in a specific
  way of producing the result.
- **Product-first.** Identify what must be produced, evaluate
  alternative routes to produce it, *then* select the best activity
  set.
- Aligns with the PMBOK 7 distinction between **output** and
  **outcome** (product is the output that must enable the outcome).

## When this applies

- Building a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)
- Designing a delivery plan from scratch
- Reviewing a plan that feels "activity-heavy" with vague outputs

## Anti-patterns

- WBS built as a list of tasks with no explicit deliverables
- "We always do these activities" copied from prior projects
- Optimizing the schedule before the deliverable is defined

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §12 [Enable change](../pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md) (output vs outcome)
- Performance domain: [Delivery](../../performance-domains/07-delivery.md)
- Methodology: [PRINCE2](../../methodologies/prince2.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/06-focus-on-products.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/06-focus-on-products.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/principles/prince2/07-tailor-to-project.md -->
<!-- title: Tailor to suit the project -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-4.0 -->

# Tailor to suit the project

**TL;DR:** PRINCE2 (and almost every PM system) cannot be used
out-of-the-box. Tailor up-front *and* keep adapting throughout.

## Core ideas

- **Two flavors of tailoring required:**
    - **Initial tailoring** for systems whose default is heavy
      (PRINCE2 — maximalist).
    - **Continuous adaptation** even for systems that don't need
      initial tailoring (P3.express — minimalist).
- Tailoring is system design, not picking-and-choosing artifacts.
- Lack of tailoring is the source of the "PRINCE2 is too expensive"
  complaint.

## When this applies

- Adopting PRINCE2 (or any maximalist method) in a new context
- Periodic system retrospectives and adjustments
- Pushback from teams that the system feels too heavy or too light

## Anti-patterns

- Using PRINCE2 by-the-book for a tiny project
- Tailoring once at adoption and never revisiting
- Cherry-picking artifacts from PRINCE2 + Scrum + PMBOK without
  internal consistency

## Cross-refs

- PMBOK 7 §7 [Tailor based on context](../pmbok-7/07-tailor.md)
- Methodologies: maximalist [PRINCE2](../../methodologies/prince2.md)
  vs minimalist [P3.express](../../methodologies/p3-express.md)
- DSDM Coach role: [DSDM](../../methodologies/dsdm.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/02-…/07-tailor-to-suit-the-project.md`](../../../sources/underneath-the-surface/02-understanding-and-interpreting/03-prince2-principles/07-tailor-to-suit-the-project.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/index.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Guide 2020 (distilled) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Guide 2020 — distilled

Cards distilled from the canonical *2020 Scrum Guide* by
Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland, focused on **what an agent or
practitioner needs**: prescriptions, accountabilities, anti-patterns
and cross-refs. The Scrum Guide itself is short and prescriptive;
cards add anti-patterns and cross-source references rather than
re-stating.

> **License:** the Scrum Guide is released under
> [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/).
> These distilled cards are a derivative work and inherit the same
> ShareAlike license.

## Cards

### Foundations
- [Scrum definition](01-scrum-definition.md)
- [Scrum theory and the three pillars](02-theory-and-pillars.md)
- [Scrum Values](03-scrum-values.md)

### Scrum Team
- [Scrum Team — structure](04-scrum-team.md)
- [Developers](05-developers.md)
- [Product Owner](06-product-owner.md)
- [Scrum Master](07-scrum-master.md)

### Scrum Events
- [Events — overview](08-events-overview.md)
- [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md)
- [Sprint Planning](10-sprint-planning.md)
- [Daily Scrum](11-daily-scrum.md)
- [Sprint Review](12-sprint-review.md)
- [Sprint Retrospective](13-sprint-retrospective.md)

### Scrum Artifacts
- [Product Backlog (+ Product Goal)](14-product-backlog.md)
- [Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- [Increment (+ Definition of Done)](16-increment.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/01-scrum-definition.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum definition -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum definition

**TL;DR:** Scrum is a **lightweight framework** for delivering value
on **complex problems** through small cross-functional teams,
**iterative Sprints**, and continuous inspection-and-adaptation.
Purposefully **incomplete** — defines just enough rules to enable
empirical work.

## Core loop (one Sprint)

The Scrum Master fosters an environment where:

1. **Product Owner** orders work into a **Product Backlog**.
2. **Scrum Team** turns a selection into a working **Increment** during
   the **Sprint**.
3. Team and stakeholders **inspect** results and **adjust** for the
   next Sprint.
4. **Repeat.**

## Properties

- **Complex-problem focus.** Scrum addresses problems where requirements
  and approach are not predictable up-front.
- **"Developers" includes everyone.** The word is used inclusively
  for any specialist doing the work — researchers, analysts,
  designers, scientists, etc.
- **Framework, not methodology.** Defines roles, events, artifacts and
  rules; intentionally leaves **techniques and processes** to the team.
- **Immutable rules.** Implementing only parts of Scrum is possible
  but the result is "not Scrum" (per the End Note). Each element
  serves a specific purpose; removing one covers up problems and
  limits the benefits.
- **Wraps existing practice.** Scrum surrounds existing techniques,
  exposing what helps and what hinders so improvements can be made.

## When this applies

- New product or initiative with complex/uncertain scope
- Existing teams that want a baseline empirical framework
- Cross-functional small teams (≤10) that own a product end-to-end

## Anti-patterns

- "ScrumBut" — "we use Scrum but we skip [X]"
