- Treating Scrum as a complete methodology and following it by rote
- Adding a project-manager role on top of the three accountabilities
- Splitting one Scrum Team into product/test sub-teams
- Using Scrum on routine, well-understood, predictable work where it
  has no leverage

## Cross-refs

- This card: PMBOK 7 [Adaptability principle](../principles/pmbok-7/11-adaptability.md)
- Underneath the Surface: [Scrum methodology card](../methodologies/scrum.md)
- See also: [Scrum theory and pillars](02-theory-and-pillars.md),
  [Scrum Values](03-scrum-values.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/02-scrum-definition.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/02-scrum-definition.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/02-theory-and-pillars.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum theory and the three pillars -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum theory and the three pillars

**TL;DR:** Scrum is grounded in **empiricism** (knowledge from
experience and observation) and **lean thinking** (reduce waste,
keep the essentials). Empiricism is operationalized through three
pillars — **Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation** — which the five
Scrum events make happen on a fixed cadence.

## The two foundations

- **Empiricism.** Knowledge comes from experience and decisions are
  based on what is observed. Plans don't beat reality on complex
  work.
- **Lean thinking.** Reduce waste; focus on the essentials. The
  Scrum framework is intentionally minimal for this reason.
- **Approach.** Iterative, incremental, optimizing predictability and
  controlling risk through short cycles.

## The three pillars

### 1. Transparency
- Process and work must be visible to those doing it **and** those
  receiving it.
- Decisions in Scrum rely on the perceived state of the three
  artifacts (Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment).
- Low transparency → decisions that destroy value and add risk.
- *Transparency enables inspection. Inspection without transparency
  is misleading and wasteful.*

### 2. Inspection
- Artifacts and progress toward goals are inspected **frequently and
  diligently** to detect undesirable variance.
- The five Scrum events provide the cadence (Sprint, Sprint
  Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective).
- *Inspection enables adaptation. Inspection without adaptation is
  pointless.* Events are designed to **provoke** change.

### 3. Adaptation
- If process or product is unacceptable, adjust **as soon as
  possible** to minimize further deviation.
- Adaptation breaks down when people are **not empowered** or **not
  self-managing**. Both are prerequisites.
- A Scrum Team adapts the moment it learns anything new through
  inspection.

## When this applies

- Complex environments with uncertain outcomes
- Designing reporting/visibility systems for a team
- Diagnosing why a Scrum adoption "isn't working" — usually one of
  the three pillars is broken

## Anti-patterns

- Hidden Sprint Backlog or undocumented work-in-progress (kills
  transparency)
- Inspection event that produces no change (kills adaptation
  feedback loop)
- "We will adapt next quarter" — Scrum demands immediate adjustment
- Team has no decision authority → adaptation impossible
- Removing or skipping an event → loses the cadence that makes
  empiricism work

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum events overview](08-events-overview.md) — pillars in action
- PMBOK 7 [System interactions](../principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md)
  (holistic / critical thinking)
- NUPP [Always be proactive](../principles/nupp/03-always-be-proactive.md)
- Scrum Guide Expanded covers the pillars in more depth

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/03-scrum-theory/`](../../sources/scrum-guide/03-scrum-theory/index.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/03-scrum-values.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Values -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Values

**TL;DR:** Five values that make Scrum's empiricism work in practice:
**Commitment · Focus · Openness · Respect · Courage**. Without them
the three pillars collapse and Scrum becomes ceremony.

## The five values

- **Commitment** — to the team's goals and to supporting each other.
- **Focus** — primarily on the Sprint's work, toward those goals.
- **Openness** — about the work and the challenges, with the team
  and with stakeholders.
- **Respect** — for team members as capable independent
  professionals; mutually with the people they work with.
- **Courage** — to do the right thing, to work on tough problems.

## Why they matter

Values shape **decisions, steps and the way Scrum is used**. Every
choice should reinforce them, not diminish them. When the team
embodies the values, the three pillars (transparency, inspection,
adaptation) "come to life" and **trust** is built.

## When this applies

- Designing team agreements / working agreements
- Diagnosing dysfunction (which value is being violated?)
- Hiring and onboarding into a Scrum environment
- Coaching conversations during a retrospective

## Anti-patterns

- Multitasking across many initiatives (violates **focus**)
- Hiding bad news from PO or stakeholders (violates **openness**)
- Skipping the Sprint Goal (violates **commitment**)
- Manager imposing solutions on the team (violates **respect**)
- Avoiding hard conversations or risky technical work (violates
  **courage**)
- Treating the values as a poster on the wall — not as decision
  criteria

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum theory and pillars](02-theory-and-pillars.md) — values are
  the human substrate; pillars are the operational expression
- PMBOK 7 [Stewardship](../principles/pmbok-7/01-stewardship.md)
  (PM as steward, integrity, care)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/04-scrum-values.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/04-scrum-values.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/04-scrum-team.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Team — structure -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Team — structure

**TL;DR:** A single small team (≤10), three accountabilities
(Developers, Product Owner, Scrum Master), no sub-teams or
hierarchies, focused on **one** Product Goal at a time.
Cross-functional and self-managing; the entire team is accountable
for a valuable Increment **every Sprint**.

## Properties

- **Composition (immutable).** One Scrum Master, one Product Owner,
  Developers. No additional layers.
- **Size.** Typically **10 or fewer** people. Smaller communicates
  better and is more productive.
- **Cross-functional.** Has **all** skills needed to create value
  each Sprint — no external hand-offs to "complete the work".
- **Self-managing.** Internally decides who does what, when, how.
- **Single product focus.** One Product Goal at a time.
- **End-to-end ownership.** Stakeholder collaboration, verification,
  maintenance, operation, experimentation, research, anything else.
- **Sustainable pace.** Working in Sprints at a sustainable pace
  improves focus and consistency.

## Three accountabilities

- **Developers** — committed to creating any aspect of a usable
  Increment each Sprint. → [card](05-developers.md)
- **Product Owner** — accountable for maximizing product value,
  manages the Product Backlog. → [card](06-product-owner.md)
- **Scrum Master** — accountable for the team's effectiveness and
  for establishing Scrum as defined in the Guide. → [card](07-scrum-master.md)

## Scaling rule

> If teams become too large, **reorganize into multiple cohesive
> Scrum Teams**, each focused on the same product. They share the
> same **Product Goal**, **Product Backlog**, and **Product Owner**.

There is no "Scrum at scale" defined in the Scrum Guide; multi-team
setups are explicit only in this rule.

## When this applies

- Designing a new product team
- Reorganizing existing teams that have grown beyond ~10 people
- Setting up multi-team product (multiple Scrum Teams sharing a
  backlog)
- Defining job descriptions and team boundaries

## Anti-patterns

- Adding a "Project Manager" role on top of the three
  accountabilities
- Splitting into dev/QA/ops sub-teams — kills cross-functional
  ownership
- Multiple Product Owners on the same product
- Single Product Owner managing many disconnected products
  (reverses the focus property)
- Team larger than ~12 → communication overhead drowns
  empiricism
- Team that needs hand-offs to a "different team" to release →
  not cross-functional

## Cross-refs

- Underneath the Surface: [Scrum methodology card](../methodologies/scrum.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Collaborative team principle](../principles/pmbok-7/02-collaborative-team.md)
- PRINCE2 [Defined roles and responsibilities](../principles/prince2/03-roles.md)
  (different shape but same principle)
- Performance domain: [Team](../performance-domains/02-team.md)
  (centralized vs decentralized PM debate)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/`](../../sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/index.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/05-developers.md -->
<!-- title: Developers (Scrum role) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Developers (Scrum role)

**TL;DR:** Anyone in the Scrum Team committed to creating any aspect
of a usable Increment each Sprint. Inclusive label — "Developer" is
not limited to programmers.

## Accountabilities (always, regardless of skill set)

- **Create the Sprint plan** (the Sprint Backlog).
- **Instill quality** by adhering to the **Definition of Done**.
- **Adapt the plan daily** toward the Sprint Goal.
- **Hold each other accountable** as professionals.

## Inclusive scope

The skills needed vary widely with the domain. The Guide explicitly
states: researchers, analysts, scientists, specialists, and other
"developers" all qualify. The word is used to **simplify**, not
exclude.

## When this applies

- Defining job descriptions for non-programmers in a Scrum context
  (data scientists, designers, hardware engineers)
- Onboarding new team members — explain the four accountabilities
- Resolving "is this our work?" questions — if it produces or
  protects the Increment, the Developers do it

## Anti-patterns

- "Developer" used to exclude QA, designers, ops, etc. on the team
- Sprint plan handed to Developers instead of created by them
- "Done" defined externally with no team buy-in or no DoD at all
- Plan locked at the start of the Sprint and not adapted daily
- "Senior" Developers excluded from accountability for quality

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum Team — structure](04-scrum-team.md)
- [Sprint Backlog](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- [Increment + Definition of Done](16-increment.md)
- Scrum Guide Expanded uses the term **Product Developer** for the
  same role.

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/01-developers.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/01-developers.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/06-product-owner.md -->
<!-- title: Product Owner -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Product Owner

**TL;DR:** The Product Owner is **accountable for maximizing product
value** and for **effective Product Backlog management**.
**One person, not a committee.** May delegate, but accountability stays.

## Accountabilities

- Maximize the value of the product resulting from the team's work.
- Effective Product Backlog management:
    - **Develop and explicitly communicate the Product Goal.**
    - **Create and clearly communicate Product Backlog items.**
    - **Order Product Backlog items.**
    - **Ensure the Backlog is transparent, visible and understood.**

The Product Owner *may* delegate any of the above; **regardless,
the Product Owner remains accountable**.

## Authority and respect

For the Product Owner to succeed, the **entire organization must
respect their decisions**. Decisions are visible:
- in the **content and ordering** of the Product Backlog;
- through the **inspectable Increment** at the Sprint Review.

People who want to change the Backlog **convince the Product Owner**.
The Product Owner does not negotiate with everyone individually.

## When this applies

- Defining Product Owner job description
- Disputes about backlog priority — answered by PO authority
- Clarifying who can change scope mid-Sprint (only PO)
- Handling "many stakeholders, one product" — PO is the single hub

## Anti-patterns

- **Committee Product Owner** — multiple people sharing the
  accountability ("Proxy PO", "PO team")
- PO without organizational backing — others overrule the order
- PO as **scribe** — only writing down what stakeholders dictate
- Organization treating PO orders as advisory
- Stakeholders bypassing PO to talk directly to Developers about
  scope

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum Team — structure](04-scrum-team.md)
- [Product Backlog (+ Product Goal)](14-product-backlog.md)
- Scrum Guide Expanded keeps the role name and adds explicit
  guidance on stakeholders / supporters.
- PMBOK 7 [Engage stakeholders principle](../principles/pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md)
  (PO embodies this for the product side)
- PMBOK 7 [Focus on value principle](../principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/02-product-owner.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/02-product-owner.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/07-scrum-master.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Master -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Master

**TL;DR:** The Scrum Master is **accountable for establishing Scrum
as defined in the Guide** and **for the Scrum Team's
effectiveness**. A **true leader who serves** — the team, the
Product Owner, and the wider organization.

## Two top-level accountabilities

1. **Establish Scrum** (Guide-correct) by helping everyone — team
   and organization — understand Scrum theory and practice.
2. **Team effectiveness** by enabling the team to improve its
   practices within the Scrum framework.

## Three categories of service

### Serves the **Scrum Team**
- Coach the team in **self-management** and **cross-functionality**.
- Help the team focus on creating high-value Increments meeting
  the **Definition of Done**.
- Cause **removal of impediments** to progress.
- Ensure all Scrum events take place, are positive and productive,
  and stay within the **timebox**.

### Serves the **Product Owner**
- Help find techniques for **effective Product Goal definition** and
  **Product Backlog management**.
- Help the team understand the need for **clear and concise**
  Product Backlog items.
- Help establish **empirical product planning** for complex
  environments.
- Facilitate **stakeholder collaboration** as requested or needed.

### Serves the **organization**
- **Lead, train, coach** the organization in its Scrum adoption.
- **Plan and advise** Scrum implementations.
- Help everyone understand and enact an empirical approach for
  complex work.
- **Remove barriers** between stakeholders and Scrum Teams.

## When this applies

- Hiring or appointing a Scrum Master
- Setting Scrum Master objectives / performance reviews
- Resolving conflicts between team and external stakeholders
- Diagnosing why a Scrum adoption is stuck (which service is
  missing?)

## Anti-patterns

- **Scrum Master as secretary / note-taker** for the team
- **Scrum Master as boss** — telling Developers what to do
- **Solo Scrum Master** focused only inside the team — ignores
  the Product Owner and organizational service
- **Permanent impediment list** with no removal action
- **Skipping Scrum events** "to save time" — violates the
  cadence-establishing accountability
- **Tolerating** organization-level violations of Scrum (e.g.
  Backlog reordered by a manager) instead of coaching against them

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum Team — structure](04-scrum-team.md)
- [Events — overview](08-events-overview.md) — SM ensures all
  events take place
- [Product Owner](06-product-owner.md) — SM serves PO too
- PMBOK 7 [Stewardship principle](../principles/pmbok-7/01-stewardship.md)
  (PM-as-facilitator is similar in spirit)
- PMBOK 7 [Leadership principle](../principles/pmbok-7/06-leadership.md)
  (servant leadership ≈ SM model)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/03-scrum-master.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/05-scrum-team/03-scrum-master.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/08-events-overview.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum events — overview -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum events — overview

**TL;DR:** Five events. The **Sprint** is a container for the other
four (**Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint
Retrospective**). Each event is a **formal opportunity to inspect
and adapt** an artifact. Skipping or skimping an event = lost
inspect/adapt opportunity.

## Why events exist

- They **operationalize the three pillars** (transparency,
  inspection, adaptation).
- They **create regularity** so teams don't need ad-hoc meetings.
- All events should ideally happen at **the same time and place** to
  reduce complexity.

## The five events

| # | Event | Timebox (1-month Sprint) | Purpose |
|---|-------|--------------------------|---------|
| 1 | [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md) | up to **1 month** | Container; turns ideas into value |
| 2 | [Sprint Planning](10-sprint-planning.md) | up to **8 h** | Plan the Sprint: Why / What / How |
| 3 | [Daily Scrum](11-daily-scrum.md) | **15 min** | Inspect progress toward Sprint Goal; adapt the day's plan |
| 4 | [Sprint Review](12-sprint-review.md) | up to **4 h** | Inspect outcome with stakeholders; adjust Backlog |
| 5 | [Sprint Retrospective](13-sprint-retrospective.md) | up to **3 h** | Inspect process; plan improvements |

For shorter Sprints, all timeboxes scale down proportionally.

## When this applies

- Designing a Scrum cadence calendar
- Resolving "we don't have time for [event]" pushback
- Onboarding stakeholders to the Sprint Review

## Anti-patterns

- Cutting events to "make room" for more work
- Daily Scrum as a status meeting for the manager
- Sprint Review as one-way demo (not a working session)
- Retrospective skipped or kept superficial — kills improvement loop
- Events at random times — destroys regularity

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum theory and pillars](02-theory-and-pillars.md)
- NUPP [Repeatable elements](../principles/nupp/06-repeatable-elements.md)
  (cadence as repeatable element)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/index.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/09-the-sprint.md -->
<!-- title: The Sprint -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# The Sprint

**TL;DR:** Fixed-length timebox of **one month or less** that
contains all Scrum work. **Heartbeat of Scrum** — turns ideas into
value. New Sprint starts immediately after the previous one ends.

## Properties

- **Fixed length.** One month max. Consistency matters.
- **No gap.** A new Sprint starts immediately after the last ends.
- **Contains all other events** (Planning, Daily, Review, Retro).
- **Each Sprint is a short project.**

## Rules during a Sprint

- **No changes** that would endanger the Sprint Goal.
- **Quality does not decrease.**
- **Product Backlog is refined** as needed (continuous activity).
- **Scope may be clarified** and renegotiated **with the Product
  Owner** as the team learns more.

## Length trade-off

- **Shorter** Sprints → more learning cycles, lower risk per cycle.
- **Longer** Sprints → Sprint Goal may go invalid, complexity rises,
  risk grows.
- Inspection and adaptation must happen **at least every calendar
  month**.

## Cancellation

- A Sprint can be cancelled **only if the Sprint Goal becomes
  obsolete**.
- **Only the Product Owner** has the authority to cancel.

## Forecasting

- Burn-down, burn-up, cumulative flow are useful but **don't
  replace empiricism**.
- In complex environments, only what has happened informs forward
  decisions.

## When this applies

- Choosing Sprint length (typically 1-, 2-, 3-week)
- Handling mid-Sprint scope-change requests
- Deciding when to cancel a Sprint
- Planning the Scrum cadence calendar

## Anti-patterns

- **Variable-length Sprints** to "fit the work"
- Trading quality for speed inside a Sprint
- Stakeholders changing scope mid-Sprint without negotiating with PO
- Cancelling a Sprint by manager fiat (only PO can)
- Sprint length > 1 month
- "Hardening" or "release" Sprint as a separate phase

## Cross-refs

- [Sprint Planning](10-sprint-planning.md)
- [Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- PRINCE2 [Manage by stages](../principles/prince2/04-manage-by-stages.md)
  (different shape; same intuition)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/01-the-sprint.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/01-the-sprint.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/10-sprint-planning.md -->
<!-- title: Sprint Planning -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Sprint Planning

**TL;DR:** Initiates the Sprint by laying out the work. Whole-team
collaboration. Output = **Sprint Goal + Sprint Backlog**. Timebox
**8 h** for a 1-month Sprint.

## Three topics

### 1. **Why** is this Sprint valuable?
- **Product Owner** proposes how the product can increase value/
  utility this Sprint.
- Whole team collaborates to define the **Sprint Goal**.
- The **Sprint Goal must be finalized before the end of Sprint
  Planning**.

### 2. **What** can be Done this Sprint?
- **Developers** select Product Backlog items in discussion with
  the PO.
- Items may be refined during the discussion (improves understanding
  and confidence).
- Forecast quality grows with knowledge of: past performance,
  upcoming capacity, **Definition of Done**.

### 3. **How** will the chosen work get done?
- **Developers** plan how to turn each selected item into an
  Increment that meets the **Definition of Done**.
- Often by decomposing items into work of **one day or less**.
- **No one else tells Developers how** to do this.

## Output

> **Sprint Backlog** = Sprint Goal + selected Product Backlog items + plan for delivering them.

## Timebox

- **Up to 8 hours** for a one-month Sprint.
- Shorter Sprints → usually shorter planning.

## Participants

- Whole Scrum Team.
- Others may be invited for **advice** (not for command).

## When this applies

- Start of every Sprint
- Re-planning after a major change or cancellation
- Designing planning facilitation for the team

## Anti-patterns

- **No Sprint Goal** at end of planning — most common dysfunction
- PO dictating *how* the Developers will work
- Planning more items than the Definition of Done can sustain
- "We'll figure out the goal later" — invalidates the focus
- Planning skipped because "we know what to do"
- Stakeholders dictating scope at planning instead of advising

## Cross-refs

- [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md)
- [Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- [Product Backlog (+ Product Goal)](14-product-backlog.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/02-sprint-planning.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/02-sprint-planning.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/11-daily-scrum.md -->
<!-- title: Daily Scrum -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Daily Scrum

**TL;DR:** **15-minute event for Developers**. Inspect progress
toward the Sprint Goal; produce an actionable plan for the next day.

## Properties

- **Timebox: 15 minutes**, every working day of the Sprint.
- **Same time and place** every day to reduce complexity.
- **For the Developers.** PO and SM attend only as Developers — i.e.
  if they are actively working on Sprint Backlog items.
- **No prescribed format.** Developers choose technique and structure
  as long as they:
    - focus on **progress toward the Sprint Goal**, and
    - produce an **actionable plan for the next day**.

## Effects

- Improves **communication**.
- Surfaces **impediments**.
- Drives **quick decisions**.
- Eliminates the need for many other meetings.

## Not the only adjustment point

Developers may meet **throughout the day** for more detailed
discussions about adapting or replanning the Sprint's remaining
work. The Daily Scrum is the **minimum** cadence, not the only
chance.

## When this applies

- Daily replanning of who-does-what against the Sprint Goal
- Surfacing impediments early
- Designing async or distributed-team variants (still 15 min, still
  Sprint-Goal-focused)

## Anti-patterns

- "Status update for the manager" / Scrum Master
- Three-questions ritual (yesterday/today/blockers) without
  reference to the Sprint Goal
- Longer than 15 minutes — split off discussions afterwards
- PO or SM dominating the meeting
- Daily Scrum as the *only* sync — Developers should also meet
  ad-hoc as needed

## Cross-refs

- [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md)
- [Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- Performance domain: [Project Work](../performance-domains/05-project-work.md)
  (follow-up items pattern is broader)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/03-daily-scrum.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/03-daily-scrum.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/12-sprint-review.md -->
<!-- title: Sprint Review -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Sprint Review

**TL;DR:** Inspect the **outcome** of the Sprint with stakeholders
and decide what to do next. **Working session, not a demo.** Timebox
**4 h** for a one-month Sprint.

## Purpose

- **Inspect** the outcome of the Sprint.
- **Determine future adaptations.**
- **Discuss** progress toward the Product Goal.

## What happens

- Scrum Team and stakeholders **review** what was accomplished and
  what changed in the environment.
- Attendees **collaborate on what to do next**.
- The **Product Backlog may be adjusted** to meet new opportunities.
- Avoid limiting the event to a **presentation** — it is a working
  session.

## Position and timebox

- **Second-to-last** event of the Sprint (Retrospective is last).
- **Up to 4 hours** for a one-month Sprint; less for shorter.

## When this applies

- Every Sprint
- Stakeholder communication / managing expectations
- Adjusting Product Backlog based on new market info
- Demonstrating progress toward the Product Goal

## Anti-patterns

- **One-way demo** — team shows; stakeholders silently watch
- Skipped because "stakeholders aren't available"
- Stakeholders use it to dictate the next Sprint's content (PO
  decides; review is collaborative input)
- Treating the Sprint Review as a release **gate** — release should
  not depend on it (see Increment card)
- "Review" only of what was completed; ignoring environmental
  changes

## Cross-refs

- [Increment (+ Definition of Done)](16-increment.md) — Sprint Review
  presents the Increment; release isn't gated by it
- [Product Backlog (+ Product Goal)](14-product-backlog.md) — gets
  adjusted based on review input
- [Sprint Retrospective](13-sprint-retrospective.md) — comes next
- PMBOK 7 [Engage stakeholders](../principles/pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/04-sprint-review.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/04-sprint-review.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/13-sprint-retrospective.md -->
<!-- title: Sprint Retrospective -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Sprint Retrospective

**TL;DR:** Plan ways to **increase quality and effectiveness**.
Final event of the Sprint. Timebox **3 h** for a one-month Sprint.

## Purpose

To plan ways to **increase quality and effectiveness**.

## Inspection scope

- Individuals
- Interactions
- Processes
- Tools
- The **Definition of Done**

Inspected items vary with the domain.

## What the team does

- Discuss what went well, what problems occurred, and how those
  problems were (or were not) solved.
- **Identify assumptions** that led the team astray; **explore
  their origins**.
- **Identify the most helpful changes** to improve effectiveness.
- **Address the most impactful improvements as soon as possible** —
  may even be added to the **Sprint Backlog for the next Sprint**.

## Position and timebox

- **Last event of the Sprint.**
- **Up to 3 hours** for a one-month Sprint; shorter for shorter Sprints.

## When this applies

- Every Sprint
- After incidents or quality regressions
- When the team feels stuck — designed for surfacing it

## Anti-patterns

- Skipped because "nothing to improve" or "we're too busy"
- Ritual-only retrospective (same template, same vague items)
- Improvements identified but never acted on
- Improvements **never** make it into a Sprint Backlog
- Manager attends and people stop being open
- Treating the retrospective as a **complaints session** without
  generating concrete actions

## Cross-refs

- [Sprint Review](12-sprint-review.md) — comes just before
- [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md) — retro concludes the Sprint
- [Increment (+ Definition of Done)](16-increment.md) — DoD is in
  scope for retro
- PMBOK 7 [Build quality in](../principles/pmbok-7/08-quality.md)
- PRINCE2 [Learn from experience](../principles/prince2/02-learn-from-experience.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/05-sprint-retrospective.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/06-scrum-events/05-sprint-retrospective.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/14-product-backlog.md -->
<!-- title: Product Backlog (+ Product Goal) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Product Backlog (+ Product Goal)

**TL;DR:** **Single source of work** undertaken by the Scrum Team —
emergent, ordered list. Its **commitment** is the **Product Goal**:
the long-term objective the team plans against.

## Properties

- **Single source of work** for the Scrum Team — nothing the team
  does originates outside it.
- **Emergent** — evolves continuously.
- **Ordered** — the PO decides ordering (see PO card).
- **Refinement** is **ongoing**: break items down, add description,
  order, size. Attributes vary by domain.
- An item is **"ready"** for Sprint Planning when the team can Done
  it within one Sprint — typically achieved through refinement.
- **Sizing is owned by Developers** (those who will do the work).
  PO may help with trade-offs.

## Commitment: Product Goal

- **Long-term objective** of the Scrum Team.
- **Lives inside** the Product Backlog.
- The rest of the Backlog **emerges to define "what" will fulfill**
  the Goal.
- A team **fulfills (or abandons)** one Product Goal **before**
  taking on the next.
- *"A product is a vehicle to deliver value. It has a clear
  boundary, known stakeholders, well-defined users or customers.
  A product could be a service, a physical product, or something
  more abstract."*

## When this applies

- Setting up a new product / new team
- Coaching teams on refinement (when, how often, who)
- Resolving "how big is this item?" disputes — Developers size
- Aligning stakeholders around the Product Goal

## Anti-patterns

- **No Product Goal** — Backlog becomes a wishlist
- **Multiple parallel Product Goals** — splits team focus
- Backlog ordered by anyone other than the PO
- Items dropped into Sprint Planning **without refinement** —
  forecasts become guesses
- PO sizes items instead of Developers
- "Backlog grooming" as one-off event rather than ongoing activity

## Cross-refs

- [Product Owner](06-product-owner.md) — owns Backlog management
- [Sprint Planning](10-sprint-planning.md) — items selected here
- [Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)](15-sprint-backlog.md)
- PRINCE2 [Focus on products](../principles/prince2/06-focus-on-products.md)
- Performance domain: [Delivery](../performance-domains/07-delivery.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/01-product-backlog.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/01-product-backlog.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/15-sprint-backlog.md -->
<!-- title: Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Sprint Backlog (+ Sprint Goal)

**TL;DR:** A plan **by and for the Developers**. Equation:
**Sprint Goal (why) + selected Product Backlog items (what) + plan
to deliver (how)**. Its **commitment** is the **Sprint Goal**.

## Composition

- **Sprint Goal** — the **why**.
- **Selected Product Backlog items** — the **what**.
- **Actionable plan for delivering** the Increment — the **how**.

## Properties

- **By and for the Developers** — they author and own it.
- **Highly visible, real-time picture** of the Sprint's planned work.
- **Updated throughout the Sprint** as more is learned.
- Should have **enough detail** to inspect progress in the **Daily
  Scrum**.

## Commitment: Sprint Goal

- **Single objective** for the Sprint.
- **Commitment by the Developers**, but provides **flexibility** in
  how the work is done.
- Creates **coherence and focus** — encourages working together
  rather than on separate initiatives.
- **Created during Sprint Planning** and added to the Sprint Backlog.
- **Kept in mind throughout the Sprint** as work progresses.
- If work turns out different than expected, the team **negotiates
  scope** of the Sprint Backlog **with the PO** — *without* changing
  the Sprint Goal.

## When this applies

- Designing the Sprint Backlog format/tooling
- Mid-Sprint scope renegotiation (preserve Goal, change scope)
- Daily inspection of progress in the Daily Scrum

## Anti-patterns

- Sprint Backlog **owned by PO or SM** instead of Developers
- **No Sprint Goal** — Sprint becomes a list of disconnected items
- Multiple Sprint Goals — splits focus
- Sprint Goal **changed mid-Sprint** instead of keeping the goal
  fixed and renegotiating scope
- Sprint Backlog frozen at planning, no daily updates
- Sprint Backlog kept in a tool with limited team visibility

## Cross-refs

- [Sprint Planning](10-sprint-planning.md) — produces this
- [Daily Scrum](11-daily-scrum.md) — inspects against this
- [Developers](05-developers.md) — own this
- [The Sprint](09-the-sprint.md) — scope-renegotiation rules

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/02-sprint-backlog.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/02-sprint-backlog.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide/16-increment.md -->
<!-- title: Increment (+ Definition of Done) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Increment (+ Definition of Done)

**TL;DR:** A **concrete, usable, additive stepping stone** toward
the Product Goal. Its **commitment** is the **Definition of Done**:
the formal description of "Done" quality. **Multiple Increments**
may be created within a Sprint and **released independently** of
the Sprint Review.

## Increment properties

- **Concrete stepping stone** toward the Product Goal.
- **Additive** to all prior Increments — they all work together.
- **Thoroughly verified.**
- **Must be usable** to provide value.
- **Multiple Increments per Sprint** are possible. The **sum** is
  presented at the Sprint Review (supports empiricism).
- **Can be released before the Sprint Review.** *"The Sprint Review
  should never be considered a gate to releasing value."*

## Commitment: Definition of Done

- **Formal description** of the state of the Increment when it
  meets the **quality measures** required for the product.
- *"The moment a Product Backlog item meets the Definition of Done,
  an Increment is born."*
- Creates **transparency** — everyone has a shared understanding of
  what was completed.
- An item that **doesn't meet DoD** **cannot be released or
  presented at the Sprint Review** — it returns to the Product
  Backlog.

## DoD scope rules

- If the **organization has a DoD standard**, all Scrum Teams must
  follow it as a **minimum**.
- Otherwise, the **Scrum Team creates a DoD appropriate for the
  product**.
- **Developers conform** to the DoD.
- If multiple Scrum Teams work on **one product**, they **mutually
  define and comply with the same DoD**.

## When this applies

- Defining quality criteria for a new product
- Resolving "is this done?" arguments
- Multi-team product setup — single shared DoD
- Choosing release cadence (release per Increment, not per Sprint)

## Anti-patterns

- **No Definition of Done** — "done" becomes subjective
- DoD too weak — Increments ship that aren't actually usable
- DoD frozen forever — should evolve with the product (see retro)
- Treating Sprint Review as a release gate — Increments may release
  earlier
- Multiple teams on one product, **different DoDs** — fragmentation
- "Done-done" / "really done" suffixes — symptom of weak DoD

## Cross-refs

- [Sprint Review](12-sprint-review.md) — Increments presented; not
  a release gate
- [Sprint Retrospective](13-sprint-retrospective.md) — DoD is
  inspected
- [Developers](05-developers.md) — accountable for DoD adherence
- PMBOK 7 [Build quality in](../principles/pmbok-7/08-quality.md)
- Performance domain: [Delivery](../performance-domains/07-delivery.md)
  (Increment definition matches book's framing exactly)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/03-increment.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide/07-scrum-artifacts/03-increment.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/index.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Guide Expansion Pack (v2026.1) — distilled -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Guide Expansion Pack (v2026.1) — distilled

Cards distilled from the *Scrum Guide Expansion Pack* (v2026.1) by
Ralph Jocham, John Coleman and Jeff Sutherland — an explicit
**adaptation** of the 2020 Scrum Guide that adds theory, expands
roles, and refines artifacts and commitments.

> **License:** CC BY-SA 4.0. These cards are derivative; same license
> applies. The Expansion Pack is **not** the canonical Scrum Guide —
> for the immutable framework see the [2020 Scrum Guide](../scrum-guide/index.md).

> **How to read these cards.** SGE assumes you already know Scrum.
> These distilled cards focus on **what's new or different** from the
> 2020 Scrum Guide. For unchanged elements, follow the cross-ref to
> the corresponding [Scrum Guide 2020 card](../scrum-guide/index.md).

## Cards

### Foundations
- [Purpose and key departures from SG2020](01-purpose-and-departures.md)
- [Foundational theory](02-foundational-theory.md) — complexity,
  emergence, self-managing, professionalism, lean, empiricism, cadence
- [Additional theory](03-additional-theory.md) — product thinking,
  systems thinking, discovery, leadership, first principles,
  people & change

### Roles (four in SGE — Stakeholder added)
- [Stakeholder](04-stakeholder.md) — *new role*
- [Supporter](05-supporter.md) — *new role* (Stakeholder subtype)
- [Artificial Intelligence](06-artificial-intelligence.md) — *new participant*
- [Product Developer](07-product-developer.md) — *renamed* from "Developers"; extended
- [Product Owner — extensions](08-product-owner.md) — what SGE adds beyond SG2020
- [Scrum Master — extensions](09-scrum-master.md) — what SGE adds beyond SG2020

### Artifacts and commitments
- [Product (artifact)](10-product-and-vision.md) — *new artifact*; Product Vision
- [Definition of Outcome Done](11-definition-of-outcome-done.md) — *new commitment* (Product-level)
- [Definition of Output Done (Increment)](12-definition-of-output-done.md) — *renamed* DoD
- [Product Backlog extensions](13-product-backlog-extensions.md) —
  PBI, Acceptance Criteria, Outcome Criteria, Refinement
- [Sprint Backlog and Sprint Goal — extensions](14-sprint-backlog-extensions.md)

### Events
- [Events — SGE extensions](15-events-extensions.md) — Sprint
  Planning Why/What/How sub-topics, refinement as event, etc.

## Cross-source navigation

- [Scrum Guide 2020 — distilled](../scrum-guide/index.md) — base framework
- [Underneath the Surface — Scrum methodology card](../methodologies/scrum.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/01-purpose-and-departures.md -->
<!-- title: Purpose and key departures from SG2020 -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Purpose and key departures from SG2020

**TL;DR:** The Expansion Pack (SGEP) is **not** the immutable Scrum
Guide. It explains the *what* and *why* "for the current times",
**evolves regularly**, and **explicitly departs** from SG2020 in
several places — those departures are flagged in the source.

## Purpose

- Explain the **what** and **why** of each Scrum element from a 2025+
  perspective.
- Assume reader **fluency with Scrum**; recommends reading SG2020 first.
- Includes references for further exploration.
- Targets product delivery by self-managing teams across many
  domains (engineers, programmers, researchers, analysts, lawyers,
  marketers, scientists).

## Key departures from SG2020 (catalog)

| # | Element | SG2020 | SGEP |
|---|---------|--------|------|
| 1 | Stakeholder | mentioned 14 times, never defined | **defined as a 4th role** with subtypes |
| 2 | Supporter | not a concept | **introduced** as a Stakeholder subtype / change agent |
| 3 | "Developer" | umbrella term | **renamed Product Developer**; may be human or automated |
| 4 | AI | not addressed | **explicitly covered** as participant ("human in the loop") |
| 5 | Product | implicit | **explicit artifact**; can be experience or platform |
| 6 | Product Vision | not present | **introduced** alongside Product Goal |
| 7 | Value | generic | **stakeholder value** — perceived need; assumption until evidence |
| 8 | Outcome ↔ Output | not separated | **two distinct commitments**: Definition of Outcome Done (Product level) + Definition of Output Done (Increment level — renamed from DoD) |
| 9 | Acceptance Criteria | not addressed | **explicitly defined** (with Outcome Criteria as subset) |
| 10 | Refinement | brief | **dedicated section**; can be formal event |
| 11 | Sprint Goal ownership | Developers | **whole Scrum Team** |
| 12 | Risk | not directly defined | **defined**; Scrum supports proactive risk management |
| 13 | Theory | empiricism + lean | **expanded**: complexity, emergence, self-management, professionalism, plus systems/product/discovery/leadership/first-principles/people-and-change |
| 14 | Self-management | mentioned | **explicit theory section**; bounded autonomy |
| 15 | Scrum Master accountabilities | three categories of service | **broader** — change agent across organization, AI integration, etc. |

## Stance

- Practitioners "should adopt Scrum **when appropriate**, with
  agency, urgency, courage, transparency, inspection, adaptation,
  rhythm, and resilience".
- Inspect-and-adapt should be **valued more than keeping promises** or
  serving the wrong stakeholders.
- All assumptions **can be wrong**.

## When this applies

- Choosing between SG2020 and SGEP as your team reference
- Mapping existing Scrum practice to SGEP's broader concept set
- Coaching teams that have outgrown the bare framework
- Stakeholder-heavy product orgs with ambiguous "stakeholder" usage

## Anti-patterns

- Treating SGEP as a replacement for the Scrum Guide (it is **an
  expansion**)
- Adopting all SGEP concepts at once without tailoring
- Ignoring the explicit departures — copying SG2020 ceremonies
  on top of SGEP terminology
- Using SGEP as cover for cherry-picking practices

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum Guide 2020 — distilled](../scrum-guide/index.md)
- [Foundational theory](02-foundational-theory.md)
- [Additional theory](03-additional-theory.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/02-purpose-…md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/02-purpose-of-the-scrum-guide-expansion-pack.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/02-foundational-theory.md -->
<!-- title: Foundational theory (SGE) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Foundational theory (SGE)

**TL;DR:** SGE explicitly grounds Scrum in **seven theoretical
foundations**: complexity, emergence, self-managing teams,
professionalism, lean thinking, empiricism, cadence. SG2020 only
named empiricism + lean.

## Complexity — the case for Scrum

- For complex work: **more unknowns than knowns**, expertise is
  necessary but **insufficient**, **cause and effect coherent only
  in retrospect**.
- Teams need time to **think, help each other, do rework, or pivot**.
- Cognitive diversity + empiricism handle complexity.
- Spaces shift over time (chaos → complex → ordered or reverse) —
  pause and verify which space you're actually in.
- "Product development often deals with unpredictability — Scrum is
  more useful than approaches with **delusions of predictability**."

## Emergence

- Patterns/behaviors arise from **interactions** in complex systems
  — **not** predictable from parts alone.
- Scrum doesn't tightly control emergence; it uses **enabling
  constraints** (timeboxes, roles, feedback loops) — "islands" in a
  sea of unpredictability.
- Influence rather than direct via short, parallel, **safe-to-fail
  experiments** + continuous feedback.
- Patterns: **Swarming, Stable Teams, Kaizen** identify and shape
  emergent behavior.
- "Self-management is **discovered** in the right environment, not
  designed top-down."
- Treats emergence as a **force to cultivate**, not a risk to eliminate.

## Self-managing Scrum Team

- Team checks if it's on track, takes action, decides how to work,
  resolves conflict, fixes problems internally.
- **Managers don't tell the team what to do.** If managers exist
  they should **demonstrate leadership** (not direction).
- Reliance on **non-self-managing teams** hinders complex work.
- Self-managing **team** ≠ individual self-management. The
  interplay among members is what matters.

## Professionalism

- **Excellence + accountability**, regardless of circumstances.
- "Cradle-to-grave" Product accountability: **maintenance** is
  included; ops gives engineering feedback.
- Software professionalism (non-exhaustive): Specification by
  Example, Clean Code, Unit Testing, **TDD**, Test Automation, **CI**,
  **CD**, Architecture & Design, Acceptance Testing, intentional
  testing.

## Lean thinking

- Reduce **waste**; focus on **flow of value** and **continuous
  improvement**.
- Built on **continuous improvement + respect for people**.
- Drives effectiveness at lowest long-term cost.

## Empiricism

- Decisions informed by **objective or observable evidence** in
  exploratory **learning cycles**.
- Cycle: observation → theory development/refinement →
  operationalization → testing/modification.
- **Evidence-informed** decision making is the response to
  experimentation.
- Pausing and reflecting combine empiricism + lean.
- "Inspecting and adapting should be **valued more** than keeping
  promises or serving the wrong stakeholders."
- "**All assumptions can be wrong.**"

## Cadence

- Sprint rhythm gives **clear short-term goals** + regular
  inspect-adapt.
- Builds **sustainable pace**, **predictability** and continuous
  improvement over time.

## When this applies

- Diagnosing whether Scrum is fit for the work (complexity check)
- Designing organizational change toward genuine self-management
- Coaching managers out of "tell-the-team" mode
- Defining technical-excellence expectations for SW Product Developers
- Setting an org-level definition of professionalism

## Anti-patterns

- Calling work "complex" when it's actually complicated → Scrum
  brings overhead without leverage
- Locking emergence out via heavy detailed plans
- "Self-managing" as code for **no management** at all (vs
  bounded autonomy)
- Professionalism reduced to compliance checklists
- Lean treated as cost-cutting only

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum theory and pillars (SG2020)](../scrum-guide/02-theory-and-pillars.md)
- [Additional theory](03-additional-theory.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Navigate complexity](../principles/pmbok-7/10-complexity.md)
- PMBOK 7 [System interactions](../principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/04-supporting-and-complementary-theory/`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/04-supporting-and-complementary-theory/index.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/03-additional-theory.md -->
<!-- title: Additional theory (SGE) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Additional theory (SGE)

**TL;DR:** SGE adds six more theoretical lenses beyond the
foundational seven: **product thinking, systems thinking,
discovery, leadership, first-principles thinking, people and
change**. Together with the foundational theory they form the
intellectual basis for the Expansion Pack's accountability and
artifact extensions.

## Product Thinking

- "**People consume Products**, not projects." Hence Scrum has a
  Product Owner, not a Project Owner.
- A **Product is long-lived**; a project is timeboxed and "leaves an
  orphaned Product behind."
- Product Owners trade off **here-and-now vs there-and-then**;
  short-term-only thinking creates technical debt, low morale,
  output focus.
- "Crossing the chasm" — strategy shift from early-adopters to
  pragmatic majority (focus on a niche; deliver complete reliable
  solutions).
- **Five Product qualities** within ethical boundaries: feasibility,
  usability, desirability, value, viability.
- **Technical debt** is shortcut-induced extra work that compounds
  like real debt.

## Systems Thinking

- Elements are **interconnected**; actions ripple non-linearly.
- Avoid local sub-optimizations (cutting unit cost while raising
  long-term cost; eroding quality goals; "improving a team or
  process that should not exist").
- Consider **upstream, cross-stream, downstream** effects of
  interventions.

## Discovery

- Not a separate phase — **continuous activity**.
- Multiple parallel safe-to-fail experiments to learn.
- Combines with delivery and value validation throughout the Sprint.

## Leadership

- "Ability to **influence, guide, inspire** a group toward a common
  goal **while avoiding demotivation**."
- **Genchi Genbutsu** — Go See, Listen, Understand; collect facts to
  inform decisions.
- Scrum strives for **leadership for resilience** — a **set of
  qualities, not a position**.
- Leadership at all levels and angles. **Anyone** can demonstrate
  leadership.
- PO and SM **balance leadership, authority, subtle control**:
  guide rather than micromanage; preserve team ownership.
- Product Developers demonstrate leadership via professionalism +
  goal orientation.
- Supporters demonstrate leadership via impediment removal and
  organizational coherence.

## First Principles Thinking

- Strip a problem to its **fundamental truths**; reason up from
  there rather than from analogy.
- Useful when challenging conventions or designing novel approaches.
- Pairs with empiricism — first-principles forms hypothesis,
  empiricism tests it.

## People and Change

- Change happens **through people**, not despite them.
- Disciplined emergent change: invite participation, run safe-to-fail
  experiments, dampen what doesn't work, amplify what does.
- "Powerful guiding coalition" of Supporters needed (Kotter).

## When this applies

- Designing a Product organization (not just a team)
- Resolving conflicts between local optimization and system goals
- Coaching managers and leaders into Scrum-coherent leadership
- Re-architecting processes that pre-date Scrum adoption

## Anti-patterns

- Project-thinking: timeboxed scope hand-off, then orphaned Product
- Optimizing one team while degrading the system
- Discovery confined to a "discovery sprint" before delivery
  starts (it's continuous)
- Leadership = the manager only
- Treating "people and change" as HR's problem, not the team's

## Cross-refs

- [Foundational theory](02-foundational-theory.md)
- PMBOK 7 [System interactions](../principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Leadership principle](../principles/pmbok-7/06-leadership.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Focus on value](../principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)
  (product thinking aligns)
- PMBOK 7 [Enable change](../principles/pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/07-more-supporting-and-complementary-theory/`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/07-more-supporting-and-complementary-theory/index.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/04-stakeholder.md -->
<!-- title: Stakeholder (SGE — 4th role) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Stakeholder — fourth Scrum role

**TL;DR:** SGE elevates **Stakeholder** to a **named role** alongside
PO, Product Developer, and Scrum Master. Stakeholders are **not in
the Scrum Team** and have **no accountabilities**, but their
classification, engagement, and governance is explicit.

## Definition

> "An entity, individual, or group interested in, affected by, or
> impacting inputs, activities, and outcomes."

Direct or indirect interest, inside or outside the organization,
its products, or services.

## Stakeholder examples

Customers, decision-makers, users, vendors, influencers, managers,
colleagues, leaders, **legislators**, financial sponsors, subject
matter experts, governance oversight. **Inanimate stakeholders**
(law, AI) should not be ignored.

## Subtypes (with examples)

| Subtype | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Customer** | Receives value by purchasing or selecting the Product (buyer / decision-maker / end-user; B2B2C and B2B2B distinctions apply) |
| **User** | Directly interacts with the Product; feedback essential |
| **Decision-maker** ("chooser", Patton) | Authority to approve / select / authorize adoption |
| **Legislator / policy maker** | Sets legal, regulatory, organizational rules |
| **Financial sponsor** | Funds; assesses viability / value / feasibility |
| **Subject matter expert** | Deep domain knowledge; supports without dictating to the team |
| **Governance** | Structures, standards, regulations that constrain and guide direction |

## Key concept — **satisfaction gap**

> "Difference between what Stakeholders experience now and what
> they wish their experience was."

The **gap to close**. Drives Product Goal definition.

## Engagement guidance per subtype

- **Customers / users:** regular intentional interactions with the
  Scrum Team.
- **Decision-makers:** "proceed with imperfect information; capture
  emerging feedback."
- **Legislators:** don't exaggerate or underestimate legal
  requirements.
- **Financial sponsors:** flexible attitude + flexible funding as
  new information emerges.
- **SMEs:** foster regular learning transfer to and across the team.
- **Governance:** single point of contact per area; intentional
  interactions; regular inspect-adapt of governance itself.

## When this applies

- Mapping the stakeholder landscape (a "stakeholder register")
- Designing communication / engagement plans by subtype
- Resolving "who decides?" disputes (decision-maker subtype)
- Setting up governance integration with Scrum

## Anti-patterns

- Treating "stakeholder" as a synonym for "customer"
- Ignoring inanimate stakeholders (regulations, AI policy)
- Single fixed engagement format for all stakeholders
- Governance not coherent with Scrum (surprise audits, ad-hoc
  contact across many touch-points)
- Fixed funding that can't adapt to new information

## Cross-refs

- [Supporter](05-supporter.md) — Stakeholder subtype that
  actively supports the adoption
- [Product Owner — extensions](08-product-owner.md) (PO is
  stakeholder hub)
- PMBOK 7 [Engage stakeholders](../principles/pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md)
- Performance domain: [Stakeholders](../performance-domains/01-stakeholders.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/01-stakeholder.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/01-stakeholder.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/05-supporter.md -->
<!-- title: Supporter (SGE — new concept) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Supporter — change-agent stakeholder

**TL;DR:** **Supporter is a Stakeholder subtype** — a **change agent**
who actively makes the organization **coherent with Scrum**.
SGE-specific concept, not present in SG2020.

## Definition

> "Supporting Stakeholders and change agents… part of a powerful
> guiding coalition who inspire and remove demotivating factors."

## Role behavior

- **Support the Scrum Team to thrive.**
- **Influence org workflows / processes / systems / products /
  services / work environment** to become coherent with Scrum
  adoption and emergence.
- Participate **when and where needed** or as requested.

## Examples (org-size-dependent)

Colleagues, decision-makers, financial sponsors, governance
oversight, managers, SMEs, marketing, HR, finance, procurement,
early adopters.

## Critical filter

> "Supporters who do **not empower** Scrum Teams to do what is
> recommended in this document **are not really Supporters**."

## Leadership expectation

- Executives and board members are critical for fostering a climate
  where Supporters support.
- Supporters **demonstrate leadership** the team appreciates —
  removing impediments, improving coherence between management
  processes and Scrum, supporting emergent change in a powerful
  direction.

## When this applies

- Designing a Scrum adoption / transformation
- Identifying who in the org **enables** vs **obstructs** the team
- Establishing a guiding coalition for organizational change
- Coaching managers into the Supporter behavior set

## Anti-patterns

- Title "Supporter" given to managers who continue to **direct**
  the team
- HR or finance departments assumed to "be" Supporters without
  genuine empowerment behaviors
- Scrum Master tries to be everyone's Supporter — Supporter is a
  distinct role with org-level levers
- Lack of executive cover → Supporters can't actually clear
  organizational impediments

## Cross-refs

- [Stakeholder](04-stakeholder.md) — parent concept
- [Scrum Master — extensions](09-scrum-master.md) — SM works
  *with* Supporters
- PMBOK 7 [Stewardship](../principles/pmbok-7/01-stewardship.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Enable change](../principles/pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/02-supporter.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/02-supporter.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/06-artificial-intelligence.md -->
<!-- title: Artificial Intelligence (SGE — new participant) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Artificial Intelligence in Scrum

**TL;DR:** AI may be a **Product Developer** or a tool, but **human
accountability stays**. Use the **"human in the loop"** model. AI
**supports** Scrum's empirical process — **does not override** it
or ethical decision-making.

## How AI may enhance Scrum

- **Empirical Process Control** — AI-driven analytics improve
  transparency, inspection, adaptation.
- **Cognitive Augmentation** — frees human team members for
  strategic, creative, ethical work.
- **Continuous Value Adaptation** — update / reprioritize Backlog
  based on live user feedback and trends.
- **Systems Insight** — surface hidden interdependencies for
  evidence-informed decisions.

## Hard constraints

- **Maintain clear human accountability for all outcomes.** AI is a
  supervised partner.
- **Human in the loop.** AI does not replace the three accountabilities.
- AI **supports — does not override** — empirical process control
  and ethical decision-making.
- The Scrum Team **remains accountable** for value, evidence
  assessment, and professionalism.
- *"At least one Product Developer should be human."*

## Concrete uses

- Backlog refinement assistance.
- Surface ambiguities in text.
- Continuous self-inspection for **bias, errors, unintended
  consequences**.
- Validate / adapt models and applications regularly.
- Ordering transparency in the Product Backlog.
- AI agents as team members.
- Test and challenge existing thinking.

## Evaluation criteria for AI tools

Evaluate **like any other contributor** to psychological flow and
learning:
- Does it **improve feedback loops**?
- Does it **help validate assumptions faster**?
- When it **acts**, is the action **ethical**?

## Adoption guidance

- Use **small, sometimes reversible trials** with safe-to-fail
  framing.
- Apply **inspect-and-adapt to AI integration itself**, not only the
  Product.
- Keep focus on **human dynamics of teamwork and collaboration** —
  AI is a potential aid for adaptiveness at speed.

## When this applies

- Introducing AI tooling into the Scrum Team's workflow
- Defining ethical guardrails for AI use in the Product
- Reviewing AI agents as Product Developers (must have ≥1 human PD)
- Evaluating an AI tool's effect on flow and learning

## Anti-patterns

- AI replaces a Product Developer entirely (no human PD left)
- AI outputs accepted without **inspection** for bias or error
- AI used to **bypass** retrospectives or stakeholder collaboration
- "We have AI" treated as a checkbox without measurable feedback-loop
  improvement
- AI integrated without **safe-to-fail** rollback options

## Cross-refs

- [Product Developer](07-product-developer.md) — AI may be a PD
- PMBOK 7 [System interactions](../principles/pmbok-7/05-system-interactions.md)
  (cognitive biases also apply to AI)
- PMBOK 7 [Build quality in](../principles/pmbok-7/08-quality.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/03-artificial-intelligence.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/03-artificial-intelligence.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/07-product-developer.md -->
<!-- title: Product Developer (SGE) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Product Developer

**TL;DR:** SGE-equivalent of SG2020 "Developers". May be **human or
automated**. **At least one** Product Developer **must be human**.
Accountabilities are extended.

## Definition

> "Product Developer is a role and an accountability. All Product
> Developers together should possess all the skills needed to
> create Increments. The combined skill set is often referred to as
> cross-functional."

## Properties (delta from SG2020 Developers)

- May be **human or automated** (AI agents). At least **one human**
  PD required.
- Multiple human PDs improve **cognitive diversity** for complex
  work.
- **Single-Product focus** — best results when focused solely on
  one Product.
- If PO or SM works on Sprint Backlog items, they do that work **as
  Product Developers**.
- Roles a PD may take: collaborator, creator, **champion of
  technical quality**, discovery, delivery, **value validation**.
- **Step-down rule:** a PD who is neither willing, ready, nor able
  to be a professional should step down.

## Accountabilities (always, collectively)

| # | Accountability | Notes |
|---|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Create an emergent plan in the Sprint Backlog for the Sprint Goal | Same as SG2020 |
| 2 | Instill quality by adhering to and **improving** the **Definition of Output Done** | DoD renamed; PDs improve it (SGE addition) |
| 3 | Create **at least one usable Increment every Sprint** | New explicit accountability |
| 4 | **Learning, often through data guided by Definition of Outcome Done** | New |
| 5 | Adapt the plan each day toward the Sprint Goal | Same |
| 6 | Hold each other accountable as professionals | Same |
| 7 | **Net improvement** | New explicit accountability |

## When this applies

- Defining a "Developer" role on cross-functional teams (research,
  data, design, engineering)
- Onboarding AI agents as PDs (with human-in-the-loop guardrails)
- Performance-management aligned to SGE accountabilities
- Quality review and continuous improvement of the DoD itself

## Anti-patterns

- "Developer = programmer only" framing on the team
- All PDs are AI — **no human PD violates SGE rule**
- PDs sized work but didn't **improve** the DoD over time
- PDs ignore **value validation** — only deliver outputs
- PDs not held to **professionalism** standard

## Cross-refs

- [Developers (SG2020 distilled)](../scrum-guide/05-developers.md)
  — base role
- [Artificial Intelligence](06-artificial-intelligence.md) —
  AI as PD
- [Definition of Output Done](12-definition-of-output-done.md)
- [Definition of Outcome Done](11-definition-of-outcome-done.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/04-product-developer.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/04-product-developer.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/08-product-owner.md -->
<!-- title: Product Owner — SGE extensions -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Product Owner — SGE extensions

**TL;DR:** SGE keeps SG2020 PO accountabilities and **extends
substantially**: PO is a **leader for the Product**, **must be
human**, **maximizes long-term value**, owns the **Definition of
Outcome Done**, and **trusts Product Developers** rather than
analyzing PBIs themselves.

## What stays from SG2020

- Single person, not a committee.
- Accountable for **Product Backlog management** and **value
  maximization**.
- May delegate; remains accountable.
- Decisions visible in Backlog content/order and inspectable
  Increment.

## What SGE adds

### Identity / posture
- **Must be human** (PO cannot be AI).
- A **leader for the Product**.
- "Every minute spent **not trusting** the Product Developers is a
  missed chance to think more strategically, work with stakeholders,
  or create more value."
- Behaviors: visionary, customer representative, collaborator,
  influencer, experimenter, decision-maker, champion of stakeholder
  engagement / clarity / Product quality / value realization.

### Extended accountabilities (delta)

| # | New / extended accountability |
|---|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Stakeholder engagement** — understanding Stakeholders, their power, expectations, needs, wants |
| 2 | **Continuously sense, listen, learn, adapt** |
| 3 | **Continuously balance trade-offs** — quality / speed / capability / risk reduction / value / simplicity; multiple stakeholders' competing expectations; team climate |
| 4 | **Develop and communicate the Product Goal** (SG2020 also has this) |
| 5 | **Develop and communicate a coherent Product narrative** |
| 6 | Create + clearly communicate **Product Backlog Items** |
| 7 | Order PBIs |
| 8 | Ensure Backlog transparency + understood |
| 9 | **Communicate outcomes via measures in the Definition of Outcome Done** |
| 10 | **Final say on the Definition of Outcome Done** |
| 11 | **Foster discovery, delivery, and validation of value** |

### Skills, support and step-down
- Strong **Product management skills** + **domain skills** required.
- New PO without these may need support and guidance.
- A domain SME is **not necessarily** the best PO — Product
  management + leadership matter more.
- **Step-down rule:** a PO who is neither willing, ready, nor able
  to gain Product management skills should step down.

### Collaboration model
- Collaborates with the **Scrum Team** to mutually agree on who
  does what; PO **remains accountable**.
- All Stakeholders and Supporters should **respect** PO decisions.
- PO has **delegated authority from the organization**.

### Multi-product caution
- If a Scrum Team is forced to work on multiple Products /
  platforms / projects: each of those Products / platforms /
  projects' manager should be a **Stakeholder (and Supporter)** of
  the PO. Scrum encourages staying focused on one Product to avoid
  the **"feature factory"**.

## When this applies

- Defining the PO job description in the SGE-aware org
- Coaching PO out of "spending time writing detailed PBIs" mode
- Resolving authority disputes — PO has delegated authority
- Hiring decisions: prefer Product mgmt + leadership over deep SME

## Anti-patterns

- **Committee PO** or AI PO — both prohibited by SGE
- PO as **scribe** writing detailed items
- PO without organizational backing — others overrule the order
- PO running multiple unrelated products as PO of each — feature
  factory risk
- Domain SME promoted to PO without Product mgmt skills (often
  better as Product Developer instead)

## Cross-refs

- [Product Owner (SG2020)](../scrum-guide/06-product-owner.md) — base role
- [Definition of Outcome Done](11-definition-of-outcome-done.md) —
  PO has final say
- [Stakeholder](04-stakeholder.md) and [Supporter](05-supporter.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Engage stakeholders](../principles/pmbok-7/03-engage-stakeholders.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Focus on value](../principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/05-product-owner.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/05-product-owner.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/09-scrum-master.md -->
<!-- title: Scrum Master — SGE extensions -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Scrum Master — SGE extensions

**TL;DR:** SGE recasts the Scrum Master as a **change agent at all
organizational levels** who is **accountable for the effectiveness
of the Scrum Team, Stakeholders, Supporters and affected people**.
Must be human. Anti-pattern list is unusually explicit.

## What stays from SG2020

- True leader who **serves**.
- Causes removal of impediments.
- Ensures Scrum events take place.
- Coaches self-management and cross-functionality.
- Single person, not a committee.

## What SGE adds

### Identity (sharpened)
- **Must be human.** Cannot be AI.
- **Change agent** working at all org levels and across business
  areas.
- **Leads by example.**
- Understands **complexity** and is skillful at "enabling the next
  right thing."

### Behaviors **expected**
Guide, coach, mentor, teacher, observer, impediment remover, agent
of change, effectiveness facilitator, continuous improvement champion.

### Behaviors **prohibited (anti-patterns explicit)**
> "The Scrum Master is **neither** a team administrator, status
> manager, taskmaster, rule-dictator, meeting-room booker, report
> dashboard administrator, chairperson, hero, coordinator, **nor an
> in-absentia Scrum Master leaving everything to 'self-management'**."

### Effectiveness scope (broadened)
Accountable for the effectiveness of:
- Scrum Team
- Stakeholders
- Supporters
- "Affected people"

### Service categories (SGE expands SG2020's 3 to ~5)

#### Serves the Scrum Team, PO, and Supporters
Helping everyone understand Scrum theory; enabling continuous
improvement; fostering timely intentional interactions; ensuring
suitable **Definition of Output Done**; ensuring events happen;
removing impediments; coaching toward self-management;
foster evidence-informed confidence; foster change agency.

#### Serves the Scrum Team
Formation, upskilling, continuous improvement; clear concise PBIs
that deliver value; vigilant about purposeful collaboration and
**Definition of Output Done** adherence and **Definition of Outcome
Done** focus.

#### Serves the Product Owner
Effective **Product Goal** definition + Backlog management;
**emergent Product planning** for complex work; **express outcomes
as measures via the Definition of Outcome Done**; clear concise
PBIs; **focus on value realization**.

#### Serves the Stakeholders (NEW)
Help adopt empirical approach for complex work; foster
"Stop putting items in progress; **start finishing items**"
mantra; facilitate Stakeholder collaboration; help focus on **value
realization**.

#### Works with the Supporters (NEW)
Lead, train, coach Supporters in adoption; clarify what's blocking
adoption; facilitate **disciplined emergent change**; foster
organizational change toward **ease of delivery vs ease of
management**.

#### Works with the organization
Lead/train/coach the org; plan and advise adoptions; work with
related departments; remove barriers.

### Tenure and posture
- Calling where helping others succeed is reward enough.
- Doesn't seek the spotlight; gives credit to others; takes
  responsibility for failures.
- Longer tenure helps the team toward potential — **only if** the
  PDs collectively develop self-management.
- **Parent-style behavior does not foster self-management.**
- **Step-down rule:** a SM who is neither willing, ready, nor able
  to be an agent of change should step down.

## When this applies

- Defining SM job description and performance criteria in SGE org
- Diagnosing SM dysfunction (use the explicit anti-pattern list)
- Multi-team / multi-organization adoption coaching
- AI tooling integration (SM ensures empiricism is preserved)

## Anti-patterns (per SGE, verbatim direction)

- Team administrator
- Status manager
- Taskmaster
- Rule dictator
- Meeting-room booker
- Report-dashboard administrator
- Chairperson
- Hero
- Coordinator
- **In-absentia** "self-management" excuse for absence
- Parent-style coaching that prevents self-management

## Cross-refs

- [Scrum Master (SG2020)](../scrum-guide/07-scrum-master.md) — base role
- [Supporter](05-supporter.md) — SM works *with* Supporters
- [Definition of Output Done](12-definition-of-output-done.md) —
  SM ensures suitability
- PMBOK 7 [Stewardship](../principles/pmbok-7/01-stewardship.md)
- PMBOK 7 [Leadership](../principles/pmbok-7/06-leadership.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-…/06-scrum-master.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/08-the-scrum-roles-in-the-expansion-pack/06-scrum-master.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/10-product-and-vision.md -->
<!-- title: Product (artifact) and Product Vision (SGE) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Product (artifact) and Product Vision

**TL;DR:** SGE introduces **Product** as a **separate artifact**
distinct from the Increment, and adds **Product Vision** as an
optional but supported long-term concept. SG2020 had neither
explicitly.

## Product (artifact)

> "A holistic experience or a platform. Service, physical, digital,
> or hybrid, delivering continuous value to Stakeholders."

### Two flavors
- **Experience** — solution that meets stakeholder needs;
  direct interaction; solves a problem/opportunity.
- **Platform** — architectural device / foundational infrastructure
  / tools enabling other Products to be built; scalability,
  reliability, flexibility for engineers.

### Properties
- **Evolutionary** and often **long-lived**.
- Single Scrum Team and Stakeholders need clarity at all times on
  **what the Product is**, **who the customers/users/decision-makers
  are**, and **what type** of Product it is (different types →
  different stakeholders + value patterns).
- **One Product → one Product Backlog** to maximize transparency
  and value.
- "**Manifestation of *why* we do *what* we do.**"

### Health rules of thumb (Product creates and maintains traction when)

- Sufficiently addresses **satisfaction gaps**
- Is **valuable, desirable, viable, usable, feasible, safe, secure**
- Has **professionalism built in**
- Has **compelling, clear, outcome-metric-oriented Product Vision +
  strategy + goal** — often including **intent, rationale,
  anti-goals**
- Adapts and improves to **identify, represent, measure emergence**
- Is **extendable and maintainable**

### Commitment
- The Product's commitment is the **Definition of Outcome Done**
  (see separate card).

## Product Vision (optional, supported)

- A **meaningful, long-term set of valuable desired outcomes**.
- The medium-term **Product Goal is often a stepping stone toward a
  long-term Vision**.
- "**Often a work of fiction**; none of it may be true." — forming
  hypotheses and running experiments in a direction is essential.
- Vision can be inspiring **but overwhelming** — Product Goal
  reduces overwhelm by being a tangible vertical slice.
- Inspect-and-adapt applies to the Vision too — **be open to
  adapting it** as evidence emerges.
- Several Product Goals are often achieved sequentially toward one
  Vision.

## When this applies

- Establishing the boundary of "the Product" for a new initiative
- Distinguishing Product organization from project organization
- Designing measurable Product strategy / goals / vision
- Multi-team Product setup — single Product Goal, single Backlog,
  shared Definitions

## Anti-patterns

- Treating Increment as the Product (they're distinct: Increment is
  output; Product creates outcomes once released)
- One Scrum Team across many Products — feature factory risk
- Product Vision treated as fixed truth, not hypothesis
- Vision so abstract it can't inform the next Product Goal
- Product without measurable Definition of Outcome Done

## Cross-refs

- [Definition of Outcome Done](11-definition-of-outcome-done.md)
- [Product Backlog extensions](13-product-backlog-extensions.md)
  (Product Goal as commitment)
- PMBOK 7 [Enable change](../principles/pmbok-7/12-enable-change.md)
  (output ≠ outcome)
- PMBOK 7 [Focus on value](../principles/pmbok-7/04-focus-on-value.md)

## Source

Full text: [`docs/sources/scrum-guide-expanded/09-…/01-product.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/09-the-scrum-artifacts-in-the-expansion-pack/01-product.md)
and [`10-what-about-a-product-vision.md`](../../sources/scrum-guide-expanded/09-the-scrum-artifacts-in-the-expansion-pack/10-what-about-a-product-vision.md)


<!-- distilled-chunk: distilled/scrum-guide-expanded/11-definition-of-outcome-done.md -->
<!-- title: Definition of Outcome Done (SGE — new commitment) -->
<!-- license: CC-BY-SA-4.0 -->

# Definition of Outcome Done

**TL;DR:** **New commitment in SGE**, attached to the **Product**
(not the Increment). Describes **observable evidence measures** that
demonstrate **realized benefits** = "value validation". Pairs with
the renamed Definition of Output Done.

## Definition

> "Describes the observable evidence measures (quantitative or
> qualitative) required to demonstrate realized benefits, often
> referred to as value validation."

- Can be for the **overall Product** or for a **specific goal**.
- **Often best to define measures *before* realization starts** —
  avoids biases and mistaken interpretations.
- **Outcomes** confirm **user changes**.
- **Impact** confirms **organizational results**.

## Validation channels

- **Product telemetry** (Product can measure its own usage).
- **Strategic deployment** efficacy validation for the overall
  Product.
- A **combination** of both.

## Categories (favor direct evidence over circumstantial)

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Customer outcomes** | satisfaction trends, customer cost reduction, customer jobs addressed |
| **User outcomes** | task completion efficiency, engagement with new features |
| **Product Stakeholder outcomes** | customer / decision-maker / user metrics, time to release / learn / pivot |
| **Business impact** | compliance, cost reduction, market-share trends, organizational time to release / learn / pivot, satisfaction across all Products |
| **Scrum Team outcomes** | technical capability (psychological flow, release frequency, tooling, skill, debt, capacity), climate / culture |

## Authority

- The **Product Owner has the final say** on the Definition of
  Outcome Done.
- The Scrum Master helps the PO **express outcomes as measures**.

## UX / CX debt (related concept)

> "Sum of design and implementation choices that make a Product or
> service less usable, enjoyable, or effective."

Recognize, track, address. Increment quality alone doesn't capture
this — outcome measures do.

## When this applies

- Setting up a new Product's measurement plan
- Reviewing Product strategy at the Sprint Review
- Resolving "are we delivering value?" questions with evidence
- Addressing UX / CX debt with measurable signals

## Anti-patterns

- **No measures defined** — outcomes left to opinion
- Measures defined **after** realization starts — biased
- Only **circumstantial evidence** (e.g. number of features
  shipped) treated as outcome
- Conflating outcomes (user changes) with **outputs** (features
  shipped) or **impact** (org results)
- PO doesn't actually own the DoOD measures
- Increment ships Definition of Output Done while no outcome
  evidence is captured

## Cross-refs

- [Definition of Output Done](12-definition-of-output-done.md)
